“That’s enough. I need a new computer.”
The next day, I started searching online for different laptops. At first, I thought choosing one would be easy.
But after looking at websites, reviews, prices, and technical information, I quickly realized there were far too many options.
Some laptops were cheap, but they had weaker performance and less storage space. Others were very modern and powerful, but the prices were extremely high.
For several days, I compared different models carefully.
Finally, I became interested in two laptops.
The first one was affordable and simple. The price was very attractive, and honestly it looked good enough for basic daily tasks like watching videos, writing documents, and browsing the internet.
Many reviews said the laptop was slow, especially when several applications were open at the same time.
“That could become frustrating quickly,” I thought.
The second laptop was completely different.
It was faster, more modern, and much more powerful. The screen quality was excellent, the battery lasted longer, and people online described it as very reliable for work and daily use.
The only problem was the price.
It cost much more than the first laptop.
For several days, I was unsure which one to choose. I thought carefully about my budget, my work, and how often I use my computer every day.
Then I asked myself an important question:
I realized that I use my laptop for many hours every day for work, organization, communication, and entertainment.
Because of that, reliability became more important than simply saving money.
So finally, I decided to buy the second laptop.

When I brought it home and started using it, I immediately noticed the difference. Everything worked faster and more smoothly. I could open several programs at the same time without problems, and the battery lasted almost the entire day.
Honestly, using it made my daily life much easier.
Of course, spending more money was difficult at first.
But now, after using the laptop every day for several weeks, I truly believe it was the right decision.
Sometimes paying more at the beginning can save time, stress, and frustration later.
And for me, that made the purchase completely worth it.
questions: Why did the person need a new laptop?
What problem did the cheap laptop have?
Why was the second laptop attractive?
What was the main disadvantage of the second laptop?
Does the person regret the decision?
answers_q: Because the old laptop had many problems.
It was slow.
It was fast and reliable.
It was expensive.
No, the person thinks it was the right choice.
cloze: Reliability became more important than saving ______.
The new laptop made daily life ______.
speaking: You are explaining why you chose a product.
example: “I chose the more expensive laptop because I needed something reliable for daily work.”
grammar: Used to compare products and explain decisions.

title: Improving My English
content: For several months, I have wanted to improve my English seriously. I already understand some basic words and simple conversations, but I often feel nervous when I need to speak or write.
Sometimes I know what I want to say in my head, but I cannot express it easily in English.
One day, after watching a video in English online, I realized something important:
“If I really want to improve, I need to practice regularly.”
So recently, I decided to create a simple study routine for myself.
I am going to study English every day, even if it is only for a short time. I know that learning a language takes patience and consistency, so I do not want to study only once in a while.
Little by little, I want English to become part of my daily life.
First, I plan to read simple English texts regularly. I started with short stories, easy articles, and graded readers because they are easier to understand.
At first, reading was slow and sometimes difficult. I often stopped to check new vocabulary.
But now, I already feel slightly more comfortable than before.
I also want to improve my listening skills.
Because of that, I listen to English audio every day. Sometimes I watch videos with subtitles, and other times I listen to podcasts or simple conversations while walking or cooking.
At first, native speakers sounded extremely fast to me.
But slowly, my ears are becoming more familiar with the language.
Another important goal is speaking more.
Honestly, speaking is still the most difficult part for me because I feel shy and afraid of making mistakes sometimes.
But recently, I understood something important: mistakes are completely normal when learning a language.
Now, instead of worrying too much, I try to practice speaking more often, even with simple sentences.
Sometimes I repeat expressions aloud at home or answer questions in English by myself.
It may look strange, but it actually helps.
Of course, I know improving my English will take time.
There will probably be difficult moments when I feel tired or discouraged. Some days I may not understand much, and other days I may forget vocabulary.
I remind myself that progress does not happen in one day. It happens step by step.
And honestly, I already feel proud because I started taking my learning more seriously.
In a few months, I hope I will feel more confident speaking English and understanding conversations more naturally.
I know the process will not always be easy.
But I truly believe I can do it.
questions: Why does the person want to improve English?
What reading materials does the person use?
Why is speaking difficult for the person?
What does the person do to practice listening?
How does the person feel about the future?
answers_q: Because English is important and useful.
Simple texts and short stories.
Because the person feels shy and afraid of mistakes.
Listens to podcasts and videos.
Motivated and confident.
cloze: The person wants to study English every ______.
Listening helps the person improve ______ skills.
Mistakes are normal when learning a ______.
The person believes they can ______ it.
speaking: You are explaining your goals for improving English.
example: “I want to improve my English by reading, listening, and speaking every day.”
grammar: I am going to study every day.
I want to feel more confident.
Used to describe future goals, intentions, and motivation.

title: A Problem at the Restaurant
content: Yesterday evening, I went to a restaurant with one of my friends after work. We both wanted to relax and enjoy a good dinner because the week had been long and stressful.
The restaurant was located near the city center and looked very welcoming from the outside. Warm lights shined through the windows, soft music played inside, and many tables were already occupied.
“This place looks nice,” my friend said when we entered.
A waiter greeted us politely and showed us to a table near the window. We looked at the menu for several minutes before ordering.
My friend chose pasta, and I ordered grilled chicken with vegetables and rice.
The atmosphere felt calm and comfortable, the waiter was friendly, and our drinks arrived quickly. While waiting for the food, we talked about work, vacations, and our plans for the weekend.
About twenty minutes later, the waiter brought our dishes.
But after taking my first bite, I immediately noticed something was wrong.
The chicken and vegetables were cold.
At first, I hesitated because I do not like complaining in restaurants. I thought maybe only one part of the dish was cold.
But after another bite, I realized the problem was obvious.
My friend looked at me and asked:
“Not really,” I admitted quietly. “The food is cold.”
For a moment, I considered ignoring the problem and continuing to eat anyway. But then I thought:
“I’m paying for this meal. It’s okay to say something politely.”
So I called the waiter calmly.
“Excuse me,” I said politely. “I’m sorry, but my food is cold.”
“Oh, I’m very sorry about that,” he replied sincerely.
He checked the plate quickly and nodded.
“You’re right. I apologize. I’ll bring you a new dish immediately.”
A few minutes later, he returned with a fresh plate of food.
This time, everything was hot and smelled delicious.
Again, the waiter apologized politely before leaving.
When I finally tasted the new dish, it was excellent. The chicken was perfectly cooked, the vegetables were fresh, and everything tasted much better.
By the end of the evening, I no longer felt disappointed.
Actually, I was happy with the service because the restaurant handled the problem professionally and respectfully.
The experience reminded me that small problems can happen anywhere.
What really matters is how people respond to them.
And in this case, the restaurant solved the situation very well.
questions: Why did the person go to the restaurant?
What problem happened with the food?
Why did the person hesitate at first?
How did the waiter react?
Was the person satisfied in the end?
answers_q: To have dinner and relax.
The food was cold.
Because the person did not like complaining.
He apologized and brought a new dish.
Yes, very satisfied.
cloze: The waiter said he was very ______.
speaking: You are politely explaining a problem in a restaurant.
example: “Excuse me, but my food is cold. Could you please check it?”
grammar: Used to describe problems and polite interactions in restaurants or services.

title: A Busy Schedule
content: Yesterday afternoon, my friend Lucas invited me to dinner after work. Normally, I would say yes immediately because we do not see each other very often these days.
But unfortunately, this week has been extremely busy for me.
Around 3 o’clock, I received a message from him while I was working on an important project.
“Hey,” he wrote, “do you want to have dinner tonight?”
When I read the message, I smiled immediately because I honestly wanted to go.
I missed talking and relaxing with friends.
For a few minutes, I seriously considered accepting the invitation. I imagined leaving work early, eating at a nice restaurant, and forgetting about stress for one evening.
My desk was full of unfinished tasks, several emails still needed answers, and I had an important deadline the next morning.
I checked my schedule again and sighed quietly.
“I really don’t have time tonight,” I thought with disappointment.
Still, I did not want to refuse too quickly because Lucas is a close friend, and I knew he was trying to organize something nice.
So I thought carefully about how to answer politely.
“I’d love to come, but I can’t tonight. I have a lot of work and I’m very busy.”
A few minutes later, Lucas replied.
“No problem at all,” he answered. “Work comes first sometimes.”
Reading that made me feel slightly better.
Immediately, I felt relieved because I did not want to cancel completely.
“Yes,” I replied quickly. “Next week sounds perfect.”
We checked our schedules and finally chose Thursday evening for dinner.
After organizing that, I returned to work feeling calmer.

Of course, part of me still felt disappointed because I truly wanted to go out that evening. After several stressful days, dinner with a friend sounded much more enjoyable than sitting at my desk for hours.
But at the same time, I also knew refusing was probably the most responsible decision.
Sometimes being an adult means making choices between personal life and responsibilities.
And honestly, if I had gone to dinner, I probably would have worried about work the entire evening anyway.
Later that night, after finally finishing my tasks, I felt tired but satisfied.
The evening was not very exciting, but at least I completed everything I needed to do.
And the good news was that dinner with Lucas was only delayed, not canceled.
But sometimes it is also necessary.
questions: Who invited the person to dinner?
Why did the person hesitate?
Why could the person not go?
What solution did they find?
How did the person feel afterward?
answers_q: Lucas.
Because the person wanted to go.
Because there was too much work.
They planned dinner for next week.
Disappointed but satisfied.
cloze: The person had a very busy ______.
There was an important ______ at work.
They decided to meet the following ______.
Refusing the invitation was the best ______.
speaking: You are politely refusing an invitation because you are busy.
example: “I’d love to come, but I have too much work tonight. Can we meet another day?”
grammar: I’d love to come, but I can’t.
We decided to meet another day.
Used to refuse invitations politely and organize future plans.

title: Working from Home
content: Last week, I worked from home for several days instead of going to the office. It was not my first time working remotely, but usually I only do it occasionally.
At first, I was happy about the change.

Normally, I spend almost one hour every morning traveling to work by train and metro. So on Monday morning, when I woke up and realized I did not need to leave the apartment early, I immediately felt more relaxed.
I prepared coffee slowly, opened my laptop at the kitchen table, and started working quietly.
The atmosphere felt completely different from the office.
At home, everything was much quieter.
There were no phones ringing, no people talking nearby, and no meetings happening around me. Because of that, I noticed that I could concentrate more easily on difficult tasks.
Usually at the office, I become distracted several times during the day. Someone asks a question, another person starts a conversation, or a meeting interrupts my work.
At home, I could focus much better.
I also enjoyed small things like wearing comfortable clothes, listening to soft music while working, and taking short breaks in my own apartment.
One afternoon, while working quietly near the window, I even thought:
However, after a few days, I also started noticing some disadvantages.

The apartment became very quiet sometimes.
 Too quiet.
At the office, I normally speak with colleagues during coffee breaks, lunch, or meetings. Even small conversations make the workday feel more social and energetic.
Working alone at home felt different.
By Thursday afternoon, I realized I missed those daily interactions more than expected.
At one point, I called a colleague just to discuss a project, but honestly we also spent several minutes simply chatting and laughing together.
“It’s strange without everyone at the office,” she admitted.
“I was thinking exactly the same thing,” I replied.
That conversation made me realize something important: I enjoy both ways of working for different reasons.

Working from home helps me focus better and feel calmer.
 Working at the office helps me feel connected to other people.
Because of that, I do not think one option is completely better than the other.

For me, the ideal situation would be working from home some days and going to the office on others. That way, I could enjoy the advantages of both systems without feeling too isolated or too distracted.
I think balance is the most important thing.
And after last week, I understand my own preferences much more clearly now.
questions: Why did the person enjoy working from home at first?
What advantages did working from home have?
What did the person miss after a few days?
Why are office interactions important?
What does the person prefer in the end?
answers_q: Because it felt more relaxed.
More quiet and better concentration.
Colleagues and conversations.
Because they make work feel more social.
A mix of home and office work.
cloze: Working from home was much more ______.
The person could ______ better at home.
After some days, the person felt a little ______.
In the end, the person preferred a ______.
speaking: You are discussing working from home versus working at the office.
example: “I like working from home because it is quieter, but I also miss my colleagues sometimes.”
grammar: Used to compare situations and express personal preferences.

title: A Mistake at Work
content: Yesterday, I made a mistake at work that caused me a lot of stress and embarrassment. Usually, I try to stay organized and careful with my tasks, so when the problem happened, I immediately felt terrible.

I arrived at the office early, checked my schedule, answered several emails, and prepared documents for an important client project. Because many things were happening at the same time, the morning became very busy very quickly.
My manager also asked me to finish several urgent tasks before the afternoon meeting.
For most of the morning, I worked continuously without taking a real break.
Around lunchtime, I remembered that I still needed to send an important email to a client. The email included information needed for a meeting later that day.
I opened the message, added the documents, and started writing.
But then another colleague came to my desk with a question about a different project.
After that conversation, I became distracted by several other tasks and completely forgot about the email.
A few hours later, my manager walked toward my desk looking slightly confused.
“Did you send the documents to the client?” he asked.
I checked my computer quickly and realized the email was still sitting in my drafts folder.
At that moment, I felt extremely embarrassed.
“Oh no…” I said quietly. “I forgot to send it.”
For a second, I worried that my manager would become angry or disappointed.
“I’m really sorry,” I said. “I prepared the email, but I became distracted and forgot to send it.”
Then I quickly explained everything that happened during the morning.
To my surprise, he stayed understanding and professional.
“These things happen sometimes,” he said. “But you need to be more careful with important tasks.”
Luckily, we solved the situation quickly. I sent the email immediately, and fortunately the delay did not create any serious problem for the client.
Still, the experience taught me an important lesson.
Since yesterday, I started checking my work more carefully before finishing the day. I also created a small task list to help me remember important responsibilities and deadlines.
Honestly, the mistake felt uncomfortable at first.
But now I understand that mistakes can also help us improve if we learn from them.
And next time, I will definitely double-check my emails before saying my work is finished.
questions: What mistake did the person make?
Why did the person forget the email?
How did the person feel after discovering the mistake?
How did the manager react?
What did the person learn from the situation?
answers_q: Forgot to send an important email.
Because many tasks caused distraction.
Embarrassed and stressed.
Calmly and understandingly.
To check work more carefully.
cloze: The email stayed in the ______ folder.
The person felt very ______ after the mistake.
The manager asked the person to be more ______.
Now, the person checks work more ______.
speaking: You are explaining a mistake you made at work.
example: “I forgot to send an important email because I became distracted during a busy morning.”
grammar: I forgot to send the email.
Used to describe work problems, apologies, and lessons learned.

title: Why I Like Weekends
content: I like weekends for many reasons, and honestly they are probably my favorite part of the week. After several busy days of work, responsibilities, and stress, weekends give me time to slow down and enjoy life more calmly.
The first reason I enjoy weekends is simple: I can finally rest properly.
During the week, I usually wake up early because of work. My alarm rings very early every morning, and sometimes I already feel tired before the day even starts.
But on weekends, everything feels different.
I can sleep longer, wake up naturally, and start the day more slowly. That small change already improves my mood a lot.
On Saturday mornings, I often stay in bed for a few extra minutes while enjoying the quiet atmosphere. Then I prepare coffee or tea and eat breakfast without rushing anywhere.
Another reason I love weekends is that I have more time for my friends and family.
During the week, everyone is busy with work, studies, or personal responsibilities, so meeting people can become difficult. But on weekends, schedules are usually more flexible.
Sometimes we go to restaurants, cafés, or parks together. Other times we simply visit each other’s homes and talk for hours.
Those moments are important to me because they help me feel connected to the people I care about.
Last weekend, for example, I met two friends at a small café near the river. We stayed there almost the entire afternoon talking, laughing, and relaxing together.
After that meeting, I felt much happier and less stressed.
Weekends also give me time to do activities I personally enjoy.
During the week, I often feel too tired for hobbies after work. But on weekends, I finally have enough time and energy.
Sometimes I read books, go for long walks, watch movies, listen to music, or cook something special at home. Occasionally, I also spend time organizing my apartment or planning the next week calmly.
Those simple activities may not seem very exciting, but they help me relax mentally.
For me, weekends are not only about having free time.
They are also important for balance.
Without weekends, life would probably feel too stressful and repetitive. Resting, seeing friends, and enjoying hobbies help me feel healthier and more motivated for the following week.
Of course, weekends pass very quickly sometimes.
Sunday evening often arrives too fast.
But even short moments of rest can make a big difference.
And honestly, after a good weekend, I always feel more ready to start a new week again.
questions: Why does the person enjoy weekends?
How are weekends different from weekdays?
What activities does the person do with friends?
What hobbies does the person enjoy?
Why are weekends important for balance?
answers_q: Because weekends are relaxing and enjoyable.
The person can rest and wake up later.
Go to cafés, restaurants, and parks.
Reading, walking, cooking, and watching movies.
Because they reduce stress and help the person feel better.
cloze: On weekends, the person can ______ properly.
Weekends give more time for ______ and family.
The person enjoys simple ______ at home.
Good weekends help the person feel more ______.
speaking: You are explaining why you enjoy weekends.
example: “I like weekends because I can relax, spend time with friends, and enjoy my hobbies.”
grammar: Second, I have time for friends.
Also, I can do activities I enjoy.
Used to organize and explain opinions clearly.

title: Planning the Weekend
content: On Friday evening, Anna and Tom were talking on the phone after work. Both of them felt tired after a long week, but they also wanted to do something enjoyable during the weekend.
For several weekends recently, they stayed home because they were too busy or too tired. This time, however, they wanted to organize something different.
Anna sat comfortably on her sofa with a cup of tea while looking outside her window.
“So,” she asked, “what should we do this weekend?”
“I don’t know,” he admitted. “Do you have an idea?”
For a moment, Anna thought carefully.
“We could go to the beach,” she suggested.
Immediately, Tom imagined sunny weather, fresh air, and walking near the sea.
“That sounds nice,” he said. “But the weather might be bad.”
Anna checked the weather application on her phone quickly.
The forecast showed clouds, rain, and strong wind for Saturday afternoon.
“That’s true,” Anna admitted. “The beach probably isn’t a good idea.”
For a few seconds, both of them stayed quiet while thinking about other possibilities.
“Maybe we can go to the cinema instead.”
They both enjoyed movies, and going to the cinema felt like a perfect activity for rainy weather.
While Tom searched for movies on his laptop, Anna checked reviews on her phone. They discussed different options together.
One movie was an action film with famous actors, another was a romantic comedy, and another was a science-fiction adventure.
“I think the action movie looks interesting,” Tom said.
“It does,” she admitted, “but the science-fiction one also looks really good.”
For several minutes, they compared trailers, reviews, and movie times.
Finally, they found a film both of them liked.
“Saturday evening works for me,” Tom replied.
After choosing the movie, they continued talking for a while about work, food, and plans for the following week.
By the end of the conversation, both of them felt more relaxed and excited for the weekend.
Sometimes organizing simple activities with friends can already make the week feel better.
The original beach plan did not work because of the weather, but in the end they still found something enjoyable together.
And honestly, both of them were very satisfied with their decision.
questions: Why did Anna and Tom want to make plans?
What was Anna’s first idea?
Why did they change the plan?
What activity did they choose instead?
How did they feel at the end?
answers_q: Because they wanted to enjoy the weekend.
Going to the beach.
Because the weather might be bad.
Going to the cinema.
Relaxed and satisfied.
cloze: The weather forecast showed ______ and rain.
In the end, they chose a ______.
speaking: You are planning weekend activities with a friend.
example: “We could go to the cinema if the weather is bad.”
grammar: We could go to the beach.
Maybe we can go to the cinema.
Used to discuss ideas, suggestions, and weekend plans.

title: Asking for Advice
content: Recently, I decided that I needed to buy a new phone because my old one was becoming very difficult to use. The battery finished quickly, the screen sometimes froze, and several applications stopped working correctly.
At first, I tried to ignore the problems because I did not want to spend money on a new device.
But one morning, my phone suddenly turned off during an important call.
That was the moment I finally thought:
“Okay, I really need a new phone now.”
The problem was that I did not know which model to choose.
When I started looking online, I quickly became confused. There were too many options, prices, brands, and technical details.

Some phones had excellent cameras.
 Others had stronger batteries.
 Some were cheap, while others were extremely expensive.
Honestly, after reading reviews for one hour, I felt even more lost than before.
So I decided to ask my friend Daniel for advice because he knows a lot about technology. He always reads about new phones, computers, and applications, and he usually gives very good recommendations.
I called him in the evening.
“Hey,” I said, “I need your help choosing a new phone.”
“Of course,” he replied. “What are you looking for exactly?”
Instead of immediately recommending one model, Daniel first asked me several questions.

“How much do you want to spend?”
 “Do you use your phone mainly for work or entertainment?”
 “Is the camera important for you?”
 “Do you travel a lot?”
At first, I was surprised by all the questions.
But then I understood why he asked them.
He wanted to understand my real needs before giving advice.

I explained that I use my phone every day for work, messages, calls, photos, navigation, and videos. I also told him that battery life was very important because my current phone constantly runs out of power.
Daniel listened carefully and then started giving suggestions.
He explained the differences between several models in simple language, which helped me a lot because technical information usually feels confusing to me.
“This phone has a better camera,” he explained. “But this one has a stronger battery and costs less.”
Little by little, everything became clearer.
After our conversation, I finally felt much more confident about my decision.
I still needed time to think carefully before buying, but now I understood which options were really good for me.
Honestly, asking someone experienced for advice was probably the smartest thing I could do.
Sometimes other people help us see things more clearly.
And thanks to my friend’s help, choosing a new phone suddenly felt much easier.
questions: Why did the person need a new phone?
Why did the person feel confused?
Why did the person ask Daniel for advice?
What questions did Daniel ask?
Did the advice help the person?
answers_q: Because the old phone had many problems.
Because there were too many options.
Because he knows a lot about technology.
Questions about budget and needs.
Yes, very much.
cloze: The old phone battery finished very ______.
Daniel wanted to understand the person’s ______.
The advice made the decision much ______.
speaking: You are asking a friend for advice before buying something.
example: “I want to buy a new phone, but I don’t know which model is best for me.”
grammar: I ask my friend for advice.
Used to discuss choices and ask for recommendations.

title: Giving Advice
content: A few days ago, one of my close friends called me late in the evening. As soon as I heard his voice, I understood that something was wrong.
Usually, he sounds energetic and positive, but this time he seemed tired and stressed.

For the next several minutes, he explained his situation to me. Recently, work had become very difficult for him. He had too many responsibilities, several important deadlines, and almost no time to relax properly.
He also told me that he was sleeping badly and constantly thinking about work, even during weekends.
“I feel exhausted all the time,” he said.
While he spoke, I listened carefully without interrupting him too much. Sometimes people do not immediately need solutions.
Sometimes they simply need someone who listens.
The more he explained his situation, the more I realized he had been putting too much pressure on himself for too long.
He worked late almost every evening and rarely took real breaks.
Honestly, I was not surprised that he felt stressed.
After he finished speaking, there was a short silence.
“A break?” he repeated. “I don’t have time for that.”
“You also need to rest,” I explained. “If you continue like this, you’ll only feel worse.”

Then we talked more about possible solutions. I suggested sleeping earlier, taking short breaks during the day, spending less time checking work emails at night, and trying to relax more during weekends.
I also encouraged him to spend time doing activities he enjoys instead of thinking only about work.
But little by little, I think he understood what I meant.
A few days later, he called me again.
This time, his voice already sounded calmer and more relaxed.
He told me that he followed some of my advice. He started sleeping more, stopped working so late at night, and even took one full afternoon to rest and walk outside.
“Honestly,” he said, “I already feel much better.”
Hearing that made me very happy.
Then he thanked me for listening and helping him.
But honestly, I did not do anything extraordinary.
I simply listened carefully and tried to support a friend during a difficult moment.
Sometimes small advice and simple conversations can really help people more than we realize.
And that experience reminded me how important it is to take stress seriously before it becomes too overwhelming.
questions: Why did the friend call?
What problems did the friend have?
What advice did the person give?
What changes did the friend make?
How did the friend feel later?
answers_q: Because he felt stressed.
Too much work and not enough rest.
To take breaks and rest more.
He slept more and relaxed more.
Better and calmer.
cloze: The friend decided to follow the ______.
After several days, the friend felt much ______.
speaking: You are giving advice to a stressed friend.
example: “You should rest more and take breaks during the day.”
grammar: Used to give simple advice and recommendations.

title: Choosing a Place to Live
content: A few months ago, my partner and I started talking seriously about moving to a new place. Our apartment was becoming too small, and both of us wanted a different lifestyle.
At first, we were excited about the idea.
We spent evenings looking at apartments and houses online, imagining our future life together. But after some time, we realized we had very different opinions about where we wanted to live.
I wanted to move to the city.
For me, city life felt practical and exciting. There are more job opportunities, better public transportation, restaurants, shops, and many activities.
Because of my work, living closer to the city center also seemed more convenient. I did not want to spend several hours traveling every day.
“I think city life would be easier for us,” I explained one evening.
For him, the city felt too noisy, stressful, and crowded. He wanted calmer surroundings, more nature, and a quieter daily life.
“I don’t want traffic and noise every day,” he said. “I want peace and space.”
At first, our discussions became a little frustrating because neither of us wanted to give up our idea completely.
Sometimes we looked at apartments in the city together, but my partner always complained about the noise or small spaces.
Other times, we visited houses outside the city, but I worried about transportation and long commutes.
For several weeks, we could not agree.
Still, instead of arguing constantly, we decided to discuss our reasons calmly and honestly.
One evening, we sat together and made a list of advantages and disadvantages for both options.

City:
 More jobs, more activities, easier transportation.

Countryside:
 More quiet, more space, less stress.
Little by little, we both started understanding each other better.
I realized that my partner truly needed calmer surroundings to feel comfortable and relaxed. And he understood that my work situation made transportation and accessibility very important for me.
Finally, after thinking carefully, we found a compromise.
We decided to move to a small town located between the city and the countryside.
The town was quieter and less stressful than the city, but still close enough for work and transportation.
When we visited it together for the first time, we both immediately liked it.
“This actually feels perfect,” my partner said.
The experience taught me something important: disagreements are normal in relationships.
What matters most is listening carefully, understanding the other person’s needs, and trying to find solutions together.
And in the end, our compromise became a very good decision for both of us.
questions: Why did the couple want to move?
Why did the person prefer the city?
Why did the partner prefer the countryside?
How did they solve the disagreement?
What did they choose in the end?
answers_q: Because their apartment was too small.
Because of work and convenience.
Because it was quieter and less stressful.
They discussed their reasons calmly.
A small town.
cloze: The person preferred living in the ______.
The partner wanted more ______ and quiet.
They made a list of advantages and ______.
In the end, they found a good ______.
speaking: You are discussing where to live with your partner.
example: “I prefer the city because it is more practical for work, but I understand why you want something quieter.”
grammar: I wanted to move to the city.
Used to discuss disagreements, preferences, and decisions together.

title: A Future Project
content: Next year, I want to make important changes in my life. Recently, I have been thinking a lot about my future, my goals, and the kind of person I want to become.
For a long time, I followed the same routine every day.

I worked, came home tired, watched videos, slept, and repeated the same schedule again and again. Of course, there was nothing completely wrong with my life, but little by little I started feeling that something was missing.
I wanted new experiences, new challenges, and more personal growth.
One evening, after thinking seriously about my situation, I told myself:
“If I want my life to change, I need to start changing things myself.”
That was when I decided to begin a new personal project next year.
First, I want to learn new skills.
I realized that learning helps me feel more confident and motivated. So I plan to study every week and improve in areas that interest me.
For example, I want to improve my English because I think it is very important for communication, travel, and future professional opportunities.
I also want to learn more about technology and organization skills because they can help me both personally and professionally.
Of course, I know learning takes time and patience.
Some days will probably feel difficult or tiring. But I want to stay consistent instead of giving up quickly.
Another important goal is traveling more.
For several years, I stayed mostly in the same places because of work, responsibilities, or lack of time. But now I want to discover new cities, cultures, and experiences.
Traveling helps people grow because it changes how they see the world.
Next year, I hope to visit several new places, even if some trips are short and simple.
I want to walk through unfamiliar streets, try different food, meet new people, and experience different ways of life.
Honestly, just thinking about those possibilities already makes me excited.
Of course, I know this project will not always be easy.
Changing habits requires discipline and effort. Sometimes I may feel tired, discouraged, or afraid of failure.
But this time, I truly want to try seriously.
I no longer want to stay inside my comfort zone all the time.
Recently, I understood something important: personal growth usually happens when we try new things, even if they feel difficult at first.
That is why I feel motivated about this project.
I do not expect everything to become perfect immediately.
But step by step, I believe these changes will help me become more confident, more experienced, and happier in the future.
And honestly, I think that is already a very good reason to begin.
questions: Why does the person want to change life?
What skills does the person want to improve?
Why does the person want to travel more?
What difficulties does the person expect?
How does the person feel about the future project?
answers_q: Because the routine felt repetitive.
English, technology, and organization skills.
To discover new places and experiences.
Tiredness, difficulty, and possible discouragement.
Motivated and excited.
cloze: The person wants to learn new ______.
Next year, the person plans to ______ more.
Personal growth often comes from new ______.
The project makes the person feel very ______.
speaking: You are explaining a future personal project.
example: “Next year, I want to improve my English, travel more, and learn new skills.”
grammar: I am going to start a new project.
Used to describe future intentions, goals, and personal projects.

title: A Difficult Moment
content: Yesterday at work, I experienced a very uncomfortable situation with one of my colleagues. It was not a huge problem, but honestly it still made me feel embarrassed for the rest of the morning.
Our team had a meeting early in the morning to discuss several projects and deadlines. Everyone looked a little tired because the week had already been very busy.
During the meeting, my colleague David presented an update about one important project. He explained some delays and problems the team had recently faced.
But while he was speaking, I made a comment without thinking carefully enough.
“Well, maybe things would move faster with better organization.”
As soon as the words left my mouth, I realized the tone sounded more critical than I intended.
The room suddenly became very quiet.
David looked surprised and slightly uncomfortable.
Immediately, I understood that my comment sounded rude, even though I did not truly want to criticize him personally.
For the rest of the meeting, I could not stop thinking about what happened.
“Why did I say that like that?” I asked myself repeatedly.
Sometimes stress or frustration makes people speak too quickly without thinking carefully enough.
After the meeting ended, I noticed David quietly organizing his documents near his desk.
At first, I hesitated because apologizing felt uncomfortable too.
But deep down, I knew ignoring the situation would only make things worse.
So finally, I walked toward him.
“David,” I said quietly, “I’m sorry for what I said during the meeting.”
He looked at me for a second but stayed calm.
“I didn’t mean to sound rude or disrespectful,” I continued. “I spoke too quickly.”
For a moment, there was a short silence.
“It’s okay,” he replied. “I was just surprised.”
After that, we talked more openly about the situation. I explained that I was stressed and frustrated about the project delays, but I also admitted that my comment was not fair.
We discussed the project calmly and even exchanged ideas about how to improve communication inside the team.
Little by little, the uncomfortable atmosphere disappeared.
By the end of the conversation, everything felt normal again.
Honestly, I felt much better afterward.
The experience reminded me of something important: words matter, especially in professional situations.
Sometimes even small comments can affect other people more than we expect.
But I also learned that apologizing sincerely can help solve many problems before they become bigger.
And yesterday, I was very glad I decided to apologize instead of avoiding the conversation.
questions: What happened during the meeting?
Why did the person feel uncomfortable?
How did David react at first?
What did the person do after the meeting?
What lesson did the person learn?
answers_q: The person said something inappropriate.
Because the comment sounded rude.
He looked surprised.
Apologized and discussed the situation.
That words are important and apologies matter.
cloze: The person realized the comment sounded ______.
After the meeting, the person decided to ______.
In the end, they fixed the ______.
speaking: You are apologizing to someone after saying something wrong.
example: “I’m sorry for what I said earlier. I didn’t want to sound disrespectful.”
grammar: Used to describe mistakes, apologies, and professional interactions.

title: Coffee or Tea?
content: I like both coffee and tea, but I drink them at different moments of the day. For me, they are useful for different reasons, and honestly I cannot imagine my daily routine without either of them.
In the morning, I almost always choose coffee.
When I wake up early for work, I usually still feel tired and sleepy. Sometimes my eyes are barely open while I prepare breakfast.
But as soon as I smell fresh coffee in the kitchen, I already start feeling more awake.

Every morning, I prepare a large cup and sit quietly for a few minutes before starting my day. During that time, I check messages, think about my schedule, or simply enjoy the calm atmosphere before work begins.
Coffee helps me wake up and feel more energetic.
Without it, mornings honestly feel much more difficult for me.
One of my colleagues once laughed and said:
“You don’t really start working before your first coffee.”
And honestly, she was probably right.
However, in the evening, my habits become completely different.
After a long and sometimes stressful day, I no longer want something strong or energizing. Instead, I prefer calm and relaxation.
That is why I usually choose tea in the evening.
I enjoy preparing hot tea after dinner while listening to music, reading, or watching a movie. The atmosphere immediately feels quieter and more peaceful.
Sometimes I drink green tea, and other times herbal tea depending on my mood.
For me, tea helps create a relaxing moment before sleeping.
It also helps me slow down mentally after busy days.
Of course, some people strongly prefer either coffee or tea and argue about which one is better.
But personally, I think both are good in different ways.

Coffee gives me energy, concentration, and motivation during the morning.
 Tea gives me calm, comfort, and relaxation during the evening.
So for me, the choice really depends on the moment.
I do not see coffee and tea as opposites.
I see them as part of a balanced routine.

Some mornings begin better with coffee.
 Some evenings become more peaceful with tea.
And honestly, I think that balance works perfectly for me.
questions: Why does the person drink coffee in the morning?
What does the person usually do while drinking coffee?
Why does the person choose tea in the evening?
What kinds of tea does the person drink?
What does the person think about coffee and tea?
answers_q: Because it helps wake up.
Checks messages and prepares for the day.
Because it helps relaxation.
Green tea and herbal tea.
Both are good for different moments.
cloze: Coffee helps the person feel more ______.
Tea creates a calm ______ in the evening.
The person thinks the choice depends on the ______.
For the person, balance is very ______.
speaking: You are explaining whether you prefer coffee or tea.
example: “I prefer coffee in the morning because it gives me energy, but I like tea in the evening to relax.”
grammar: I prefer coffee in the morning.
I choose tea in the evening.
Used to describe habits, routines, and nuanced preferences.

title: Why I Exercise
content: I exercise regularly for several reasons, and honestly it has become an important part of my life. A few years ago, I was much less active than today.
Most of my days followed the same routine.

I worked for many hours, spent a lot of time sitting, and often felt tired and stressed. After work, I usually stayed home watching videos or using my phone instead of moving or doing physical activity.
Little by little, I started feeling weaker and less energetic.
One day, after climbing stairs and becoming tired very quickly, I realized I needed to change some habits.
So I decided to start exercising more regularly.
At first, it was not easy at all.
After only a few minutes of exercise, I already felt exhausted. Sometimes my muscles hurt, and occasionally I wanted to stop completely.
But I continued slowly and tried to stay consistent.
Now, one of the main reasons I exercise is my health.
Since becoming more active, I feel physically stronger and healthier. I sleep better at night, my body feels less tired during the day, and even simple activities became easier than before.
I also noticed that regular exercise gives me more energy overall.
Before, I often felt tired in the afternoon, especially after work. But now I usually feel more active and productive during the day.
Another important reason is stress reduction.
Life can sometimes become stressful because of work, responsibilities, and daily problems. When I feel overwhelmed or frustrated, exercise helps me relax mentally.
For example, after a difficult day, going for a walk, exercising at the gym, or doing sports at home often improves my mood immediately.
While exercising, I stop thinking so much about stress and focus more on movement and breathing.
Afterward, I usually feel calmer and more positive.
Exercise also improves my mood in general.
Some days, I start exercising feeling tired or unmotivated. But surprisingly, I almost always feel better afterward.
One friend even noticed the difference recently.
“You seem happier lately,” she told me.
And honestly, I think regular exercise is one reason for that change.
Of course, there are still difficult days.
Sometimes I feel lazy, tired, or unmotivated. On cold or stressful days, skipping exercise feels very tempting.
But even then, I try to do something small instead of stopping completely.
Because now I understand that consistency matters more than perfection.
For me, exercise is no longer only about appearance or fitness.
It is also about feeling healthier, calmer, stronger, and more balanced in everyday life.
And that is why it became so important to me.
questions: Why did the person start exercising?
What health improvements did the person notice?
How does exercise help with stress?
What did the friend notice recently?
Why is exercise important for the person now?
answers_q: Because the person felt tired and weak.
More strength, better sleep, and more energy.
It helps the person relax mentally.
The person seemed happier.
Because it improves health and balance.
cloze: Exercise helps the person feel more ______.
speaking: You are explaining why exercise is important for you.
example: “I exercise because it helps my health, reduces stress, and gives me more energy.”
grammar: I continue even when it is difficult.
Used to explain habits and structured personal reasons.

title: My First Presentation
content: Last week, I gave a presentation at work for the first time. Before that day, I had never spoken in front of a group of colleagues professionally, so honestly I felt extremely nervous.
A few days earlier, my manager asked me to present a small project update during a team meeting.
“At first, it will only be ten minutes,” he explained. “Nothing too difficult.”
Even though the presentation was short, I immediately started worrying.

“What if I forget my words?”
 “What if I make mistakes?”
 “What if everyone notices I’m nervous?”
For several evenings before the meeting, I prepared very carefully. I created simple slides, organized my ideas, and practiced speaking at home several times.
Sometimes I even practiced in front of a mirror.
Still, despite all the preparation, I continued feeling stressed.
On the morning of the presentation, I arrived at work earlier than usual. While setting up my computer in the meeting room, I noticed my hands shaking slightly.
I smiled back, but inside I was still very nervous.
When the meeting finally started, my manager introduced me to the group.
Then suddenly, everyone looked at me.
My voice sounded quieter than usual, and I spoke slowly because I was trying very hard not to make mistakes. I also avoided looking directly at people for the first few minutes.
But little by little, something started changing.
As I continued explaining the project, I realized people were actually listening carefully. Some colleagues nodded, others took notes, and nobody looked bored or impatient.
That helped me feel more comfortable.
I spoke more naturally, explained my ideas more clearly, and even answered a few questions successfully near the end of the presentation.
When I finally finished, the room became quiet for one second.
Then several people smiled and thanked me.
Honestly, hearing those comments made me feel extremely relieved and proud.
After the meeting, my manager also gave me positive feedback. He said I handled the presentation well and encouraged me to continue improving my public speaking skills.
That experience changed how I see presentations now.
Before, I thought public speaking was impossible for me because I was too nervous.
But now I understand something important: confidence often comes little by little through experience and practice.
Of course, I still feel nervous about future presentations.
But now I also know that I am capable of doing them successfully.
And honestly, that realization feels really good.
questions: Why was the person nervous?
How did the person prepare for the presentation?
What happened during the presentation?
What feedback did the person receive?
What did the experience teach the person?
answers_q: Because it was the first presentation.
By practicing and preparing slides.
The person slowly became more confident.
Positive feedback.
That confidence grows with practice.
cloze: The person practiced the presentation at ______.
At first, the person spoke very ______.
The experience helped the person feel more ______.
speaking: You are talking about your first presentation experience.
example: “At first I was very nervous, but later I felt more confident and finished successfully.”
vocab: public speaking → prise de parole en public
grammar: I was nervous at the beginning.
Used to describe personal experiences, emotions, and progress.

title: Why Learning English Is Important
content: Today, I believe learning English is very important for many reasons. English is used in many countries around the world, and it has become one of the most common international languages.
Even people from different countries often use English to communicate together.
For me, learning English is not only about school or grammar.
It is also about opportunities, communication, and personal growth.
The first reason English is important is work.
Many companies use English every day, especially international businesses. Emails, meetings, presentations, software, and professional documents are often written in English.
Because of that, people who speak English usually have more professional opportunities.
For example, some jobs require communication with clients or colleagues from other countries. In those situations, English becomes very useful.
Personally, I also think English can help people feel more confident professionally.
When you understand conversations, presentations, or documents in another language, you feel more independent and comfortable.

When people travel abroad, English often helps them communicate more easily. Even if the local language is different, many people still understand basic English in hotels, airports, restaurants, or tourist places.

“Where is the station?”
 “How much is this?”
 “Can you help me?”
can make traveling much easier and less stressful.
I also think speaking English helps people feel less afraid when visiting new countries.
Another important reason is access to information.
Today, a huge amount of information online is available in English. Articles, videos, podcasts, books, tutorials, and courses are often easier to find in English than in other languages.
Sometimes, important information is not even translated.
Because of that, understanding English allows people to learn more about many subjects like technology, business, science, culture, or entertainment.
Personally, I noticed that even simple English skills already help me understand more things online than before.
Of course, learning English is not always easy.
Sometimes pronunciation feels difficult, grammar becomes confusing, and understanding native speakers can feel frustrating.
There are days when progress seems slow.
But despite those difficulties, I still believe it is worth the effort.
Little by little, every new word and every conversation helps build confidence.
That is why I continue studying regularly.
For me, English is more than just a school subject.
It is a practical and useful skill that can open many doors in life.
And honestly, I believe learning it is one of the best investments people can make for their future.
questions: Why is English useful for work?
How does English help during travel?
Why does English give access to more information?
What difficulties can people have when learning English?
Why does the person continue studying English?
answers_q: Because many companies use it.
It helps communication abroad.
Because much information online is in English.
Pronunciation, grammar, and understanding speakers.
Because it is useful and important.
cloze: Many online articles and videos are in ______.
speaking: You are explaining why learning English is important.
example: “Learning English is important because it helps with work, travel, and communication.”
grammar: Also, it gives access to information.
Used to explain ideas clearly with organized arguments.

title: A Difficult Decision
content: Last year, Lucas had to make an important decision about his career. He was working for a large company in Paris. The job was stable, the salary was correct, and his colleagues were friendly. From the outside, everything looked fine.

Every morning, he woke up tired and unmotivated. He followed the same routine every day. He took the train at 7:30, arrived at the office before 9:00, answered emails, joined meetings, and went home late in the evening. After a while, he started feeling stressed and emotionally tired.
One evening, his friend Emma noticed something during dinner.
“You’ve been really quiet lately,” she said. “Are you okay?”
Lucas hesitated for a few seconds.
“I’m not sure,” he admitted. “Honestly, I feel stuck.”
“Yeah, it is,” he replied. “That’s the problem. It’s stable… but I’m not happy anymore.”

For months, Lucas had been thinking about changing jobs. A smaller company had recently contacted him. The position sounded much more interesting. He would have more responsibilities, more freedom, and the chance to work on creative projects.
But there was also a problem.
The company was young and still growing. The salary was slightly lower, and the future was less predictable. Lucas was afraid of making a bad decision.
“What if it doesn’t work?” he asked one evening while talking to his sister Sophie on the phone.
“Well,” Sophie replied calmly, “what happens if you stay where you are?”
That question stayed in his mind for days.

At work, things became more difficult. Lucas noticed that he was losing motivation. He stopped sharing ideas during meetings. Even small tasks felt exhausting. One afternoon, his manager Daniel asked to speak with him privately.
“You’re usually very involved,” Daniel said. “But recently, you seem distracted.”
Lucas did not know what to answer at first.
“I think I need a change,” he finally said.
“I understand,” he replied. “Sometimes people need new challenges.”
That conversation surprised Lucas. He expected criticism, but instead, he felt understood.

A few days later, he met the director of the new company in a small café near the river. They talked for almost two hours about projects, teamwork, and future goals. The atmosphere felt different from his current job. More relaxed. More human.
For the first time in months, Lucas felt excited again.
Still, making the final decision was difficult.

The night before giving his answer, he could not sleep well. He kept thinking about the risks. He walked around his apartment, made coffee at midnight, and looked again at the contract on his kitchen table.
Then he remembered something Emma told him earlier that week.
“You always give good advice to other people,” she said. “Maybe you should listen to yourself this time.”
The next morning, Lucas took a deep breath and accepted the new job offer.
The first few months were not easy. He had to learn new systems, manage more responsibilities, and adapt quickly. Sometimes he felt overwhelmed. Sometimes he doubted himself.
But little by little, things improved.
He became more confident. He started enjoying meetings again. He felt comfortable sharing ideas. His work finally felt meaningful.
One evening after work, he met Emma again for dinner.
“So,” she asked with a smile, “was it the right decision?”
And for the first time in a long time, he truly meant it.
questions: Why was Lucas unhappy in his old job?
What opportunity did Lucas receive?
What advice did Sophie give him?
Why was the new job risky?
How did Lucas feel after a few months in the new company?
Do you think Lucas made the right decision? Why?
answers_q: He felt stuck, unmotivated, and stressed.
He received an offer for a new and more interesting job.
She asked him what would happen if he stayed in his current job.
The company was smaller and less stable.
He felt more confident and satisfied.
Yes, because he became happier and more motivated.
cloze: Lucas felt ______ in his old job.

example: A few years ago, I decided to move to another city for work. At first, I was nervous because I did not know anyone there. My family gave me different opinions, but I wanted a new challenge. In the end, I accepted the opportunity. It was difficult in the beginning, but now I feel more independent and confident.
grammar: “I think it was the right decision.”
“I was not sure what to do.”
“I learned a lot from the experience.”

title: A Problem at Work
content: Last month, Emma faced a difficult situation at work. She had recently started a new position in a marketing company, and she wanted to prove that she was responsible and capable. Her manager, Daniel, trusted her with an important task for an international client.
“This is a great opportunity,” she told her colleague Lucas during lunch. “If this project goes well, maybe I’ll get more responsibilities.”
“I’m sure you’ll do well,” he replied. “You’re always organized.”
The project involved preparing a presentation for an important meeting. Emma had to collect sales data, organize the information, and send the final report to the client before Friday morning.
During the week, she worked long hours. She checked numbers, answered emails, and prepared slides late in the evening. She was tired but motivated.
Then, on Thursday afternoon, something went wrong.
While reviewing the document one last time, Emma suddenly noticed a serious mistake. Some financial numbers in the presentation were incorrect. She had copied data from an old report by accident.

If the client received the wrong information, the company could lose credibility. Emma immediately tried to fix the problem alone. She opened different files, searched through emails, and compared documents. But the more she worked, the more confused she became.
After two stressful hours, the situation became even more complicated.
Emma realized that correcting the mistake would take more time than expected. She also noticed another problem: some updated figures were missing completely.
She looked around the office. Most people had already gone home.
For a moment, she considered hiding the problem until the next day. But deep inside, she knew that would only make things worse.
At almost 8 p.m., she finally walked to Daniel’s office.
He was still working quietly in front of his computer.
“Do you have a minute?” Emma asked nervously.
“I made a mistake in the client presentation,” she admitted. “I used incorrect financial data, and now I’m having trouble fixing everything before tomorrow.”
For a few seconds, Daniel stayed silent. Emma expected him to be angry.
Instead, he asked calmly, “Okay. Let’s look at it together.”
They spent the next hour reviewing the files carefully. Daniel helped her identify the missing numbers, and together they corrected the presentation step by step.
At one point, Emma apologized again.
“I’m really sorry,” she said quietly. “I should have checked the report more carefully.”
Daniel nodded, but his tone remained calm.
“Mistakes happen,” he replied. “What matters is how you react to them.”
“You told me the truth early enough for us to find a solution,” he continued. “Honestly, that’s professional behavior.”
She had expected criticism, not understanding.
Around 9:30 p.m., they finally finished the corrected presentation. Emma leaned back in her chair and exhaled deeply.
“I could see that,” he said. “But next time, don’t try to manage everything alone.”
“Yeah… I think I learned that lesson tonight.”
The next morning, the client meeting went well. The presentation was successful, and no one noticed the earlier problem.
Later that day, Lucas joined Emma for coffee in the office kitchen.
“So,” he asked, “how did the big project go?”
“It ended well,” she replied. “But honestly, yesterday was terrible.”
She explained the situation, and Lucas listened carefully.
“It was,” Emma answered. “But I learned something important.”
“It’s better to ask for help early than to panic alone.”
And after everything that happened, Emma knew she would remember that lesson for a long time.
questions: Why was the project important for Emma?
What mistake did Emma make?
Why did the situation become more complicated?
How did Daniel react when Emma explained the problem?
What lesson did Emma learn from the experience?
Have you ever made a mistake at work or school? What happened?
answers_q: It was an important project for an international client.
She used incorrect financial data in the presentation.
Some updated figures were missing.
He stayed calm and helped her solve the problem.
She learned that it is better to ask for help early.
Example answer: Yes, I once forgot an important deadline, but my team helped me fix the situation.
cloze: Emma was responsible for an important ______.
Emma felt very ______ after discovering the mistake.
Together, they found a ______ to the problem.
speaking: Talk about a time when you made a mistake.

How did you feel?
