What did you learn from the experience?
example: Last year, I made a mistake during a group project at school. I forgot to send an important document before the deadline. At first, I tried to solve the problem alone, but then I asked my teacher for help. She was understanding, and together we found a solution. After that, I learned to communicate earlier when I have a problem.
grammar: ASKING FOR HELP & TALKING ABOUT PROBLEMS
“I should have checked the report more carefully.”

title: A Memorable Trip
content: Two years ago, Sophie went on a trip that completely changed her way of thinking. At the time, she was living in a busy city and working long hours in a stressful office job. Her days felt repetitive, and she spent most of her time looking at screens, answering emails, and rushing from one place to another.
One evening after work, she met her friend Emma for dinner.
“You look exhausted,” Emma said while looking at her carefully.
“I know,” Sophie replied. “But I don’t even know where to go anymore. Everywhere feels crowded.”

A few days later, Sophie found a small travel website that described a quiet village in the mountains. There were only a few hundred people living there. No shopping centers. No nightlife. Almost no internet connection.
At first, the idea sounded terrible.
Still, something about the photos attracted her. The mountains looked peaceful, and the village seemed far away from stress and noise.
A week later, she booked the trip.

When Sophie arrived, she immediately felt uncomfortable. The village was much smaller than she expected. There were no large hotels, no busy streets, and very few tourists. Even her phone barely worked.
“This is going to be a very long week,” she thought.
The owner of the small guesthouse welcomed her warmly.
“My name is Elena,” the woman said with a smile. “If you need anything, just ask.”
Sophie thanked her politely, but inside, she still felt unsure.

The first two days were difficult. She kept checking her phone even though there was almost no signal. She missed music, social media, and the noise of the city. In the evenings, the silence felt strange to her.
But slowly, things started to change.

On the third morning, Sophie woke up early because of the sunlight coming through the window. She opened the curtains and looked outside. The mountains were covered with light fog, and everything was completely quiet.
For the first time in months, she felt calm.
Later that day, she decided to go for a walk near the forest. The air felt fresh, and she could hear birds and water from a nearby river. She walked for hours without looking at her phone once.
That evening, she joined several local people for dinner in a small family restaurant. At first, she mostly listened to the conversations around her. The atmosphere felt relaxed and natural.
An older man named Mateo started talking to her.
“So, what brings you here?” he asked.
“I needed a break from work,” Sophie admitted.
“People come here when they want silence,” he said. “Sometimes silence helps us understand ourselves better.”
Sophie thought about his words for a moment.

Over the next few days, she spent more time with the local people. She learned about their lifestyle, their traditions, and their daily routines. Life there was simple, but nobody seemed constantly stressed or in a hurry.
One afternoon, Elena showed Sophie a small garden behind the guesthouse.
“We grow most of our food here,” she explained proudly.
“Doesn’t that take a lot of work?”
“It does,” Elena replied. “But it also gives us balance.”
That sentence stayed in Sophie’s mind.
Little by little, she stopped thinking about work emails and deadlines. She slept better, felt more relaxed, and even started writing in a notebook every evening.
One night, while sitting outside and watching the mountains, Sophie realized something important.
She had spent years trying to be productive all the time. But she had forgotten how to simply enjoy life.
At the end of the trip, Sophie felt emotional about leaving.
On her final morning, Mateo shook her hand warmly.

When she returned home, her life did not change completely overnight. She still worked hard, and she still had responsibilities. But she started making small changes. She spent less time online, took more breaks, and tried to create a healthier balance in her life.
And sometimes, when life became stressful again, she remembered the silence of the mountains and the feeling of peace she found there.
questions: Why did Sophie decide to take a trip?
Why was she uncomfortable at the beginning?
What helped Sophie start relaxing?
What did Mateo say about silence?
What important realization did Sophie have during the trip?
Would you enjoy spending time in a quiet village? Why or why not?
answers_q: She needed a break from her stressful life and work.
There was no internet, very few people, and too much silence.
Nature, walking, and spending time with local people helped her relax.
He said that silence can help people understand themselves better.
She realized that she forgot how to enjoy life.
Example answer: Yes, because it seems peaceful and relaxing.
cloze: Sophie traveled to a small village in the ______.
At first, she felt uncomfortable because there was almost no ______.
The local people were very kind and ______.
Sophie started to feel more ______ after a few days.
The trip changed Sophie’s way of ______.
speaking: Talk about a trip or place that had an important impact on you.
Where did you go?
What did you do there?
How did you feel?
Did the experience change you in some way?
example: Last summer, I visited a small town near the sea. At first, I thought I would be bored because it was very quiet. But after a few days, I started enjoying the slower lifestyle. I spent time walking, reading, and relaxing. The trip helped me feel less stressed and more balanced.
grammar: “I went on a trip that I will never forget.”
“At first, I was not sure I would enjoy it.”
“I learned a lot about their lifestyle.”

title: Learning Something New
content: Recently, Anna decided to learn something completely new: cooking.

For years, she had depended on ready-made meals, delivery apps, and quick snacks between work meetings. Cooking always seemed complicated and stressful to her. Every time she watched cooking videos online, the recipes looked too difficult.
“I could never do that,” she often told herself.
One evening after work, Anna invited her friend Sophie to her apartment. As usual, dinner came in a paper delivery bag.
Sophie looked at the food and smiled.
“You order food almost every day, don’t you?”
“Don’t you ever want to learn how to cook?”
But later that evening, the conversation stayed in her mind.
A few days later, Anna watched a simple cooking video online. The recipe only required basic ingredients and took less than thirty minutes. For the first time, cooking did not seem impossible.
The next Saturday, she went to a small supermarket near her apartment and bought vegetables, pasta, chicken, and a few spices. She felt strangely motivated.
At home, she opened the recipe on her phone and started carefully following each step.
She cut the vegetables too quickly and almost burned the onions. Then she accidentally added too much salt to the sauce.
“This smells terrible,” she whispered while looking at the pan nervously.
When she finally tasted the meal, she made a face immediately.
Still, Anna did not give up.
The following week, she tried another recipe. This time, she moved more slowly and paid closer attention to the instructions. The result was slightly better.

Little by little, cooking became part of her routine. After work, instead of ordering food immediately, she started preparing simple meals at home. Sometimes she listened to music while cooking. Sometimes she called Emma on speakerphone during dinner preparation.
One evening, Emma asked her, “So, how’s your new cooking project going?”
“That’s normal,” Emma replied. “Learning anything new takes time.”
At first, Anna still made many mistakes. She forgot ingredients, overcooked pasta, and once even burned rice so badly that smoke filled the kitchen.
That evening, Lucas received a message from her.
“I think I almost destroyed my apartment tonight.”
A few seconds later, he replied:
Anna smiled while reading the message.

Over time, she became more comfortable in the kitchen. She learned how to organize ingredients before starting. She understood basic cooking techniques and started changing recipes slightly to match her taste.
That made her feel more confident.
One Friday evening, Sophie came back to Anna’s apartment for dinner.
This time, there was no delivery bag on the table.
Anna looked nervous while serving the food.
“I hope it’s okay,” she said quietly.
Sophie tasted the meal and looked surprised.
“It’s actually really good,” Sophie said with a smile.
For the first time, she realized how much progress she had made.
Cooking was no longer just a difficult activity. It had become relaxing. After stressful days at work, preparing meals helped her slow down and focus on something simple and creative.
One evening, while cleaning the kitchen after dinner, Anna thought about the past few months.
At the beginning, she almost gave up after her first failure. But now, she could prepare several simple meals without stress.
More importantly, she understood something valuable.
Learning something new always feels uncomfortable at first. People often want immediate results, but real progress happens little by little.
And sometimes, small improvements can completely change the way you feel about yourself.
questions: Why did Anna decide to learn cooking?
What problems did she have at the beginning?
How did her friends support her?
Why did Anna continue practicing?
How did cooking change Anna’s daily life?
What new skill would you like to learn?
answers_q: She wanted to try something new and become more independent.
She made mistakes and burned food.
They encouraged her and gave her positive support.
She wanted to improve and not give up.
Cooking became relaxing and helped her feel more confident.
Example answer: I would like to learn photography because it looks creative and interesting.
cloze: Anna decided to learn how to ______.
After a few weeks, Anna started to ______.
speaking: Talk about something new you learned recently.
Why did you decide to learn it?
Was it difficult at first?
Did you make mistakes?
How did you feel after improving?
example: Recently, I started learning English more seriously. At first, I was not very confident, especially when speaking. I made many mistakes and sometimes felt frustrated. However, I practiced regularly and tried to stay motivated. After a few months, I noticed real progress, and now I feel much more comfortable.
grammar: “I decided to learn how to cook.”
“Now, I feel proud of my progress.”

title: A Change in Routine
content: Last year, Tom realized that his lifestyle was becoming unhealthy. At first, he tried to ignore the problem because he was busy with work and personal responsibilities. But little by little, the situation became more difficult.

Most evenings, Tom stayed awake too late watching videos or scrolling on his phone. He often slept only five or six hours per night. In the morning, he woke up exhausted and immediately drank strong coffee to stay awake.
His daily routine was not balanced at all.
He rarely exercised, ordered fast food several times a week, and spent most of his day sitting in front of a computer. Even simple activities started feeling difficult.
One Monday morning at work, his colleague Emma noticed his expression during a meeting.
“You look completely exhausted,” she whispered quietly.
Tom did not answer immediately because he knew she was right.

Later that evening, he returned home feeling stressed and unmotivated. He looked around his apartment and realized how disorganized everything had become. Dirty dishes were still in the kitchen, unopened emails filled his phone, and he had no energy left.
For the first time, he admitted something to himself:
A few days later, Tom met Lucas for lunch. While they were eating, Lucas talked about his recent lifestyle changes.
“I started exercising regularly a few months ago,” Lucas explained. “Honestly, I feel much better now.”
“Not always. At first, it was terrible.”
“But after a while,” Lucas continued, “it became part of my routine. I sleep better now, and I feel less stressed.”
That conversation stayed in Tom’s mind.
The following weekend, he decided to make small changes instead of trying to transform his entire life immediately. He knew that dramatic changes usually failed quickly.
So he started with simple goals.
First, he decided to sleep earlier. Instead of using his phone late at night, he began reading for twenty minutes before bed. The first week was difficult because he was used to sleeping very late.
Then he started waking up earlier in the morning. At first, he hated it.
One cold morning, while preparing for a short walk before work, he almost gave up completely.
“This is a terrible idea,” he thought while putting on his shoes.
The walk lasted only fifteen minutes, but afterward, he noticed something surprising: he felt more awake and more relaxed.

Little by little, Tom added new habits to his routine. He started preparing simple meals at home instead of ordering food every evening. He exercised two or three times per week, even when he did not feel motivated.
There were evenings when he wanted to return to his old habits. Sometimes he skipped workouts or stayed awake too late again. But instead of feeling guilty, he simply tried again the next day.
After a few months, the changes became more visible.
Tom felt healthier and more energetic. He concentrated better at work and felt less stressed during meetings. Even his mood improved.
One afternoon, Emma noticed the difference immediately.
“You seem much more relaxed lately,” she said.
He thought for a moment before answering.
“I stopped waiting for motivation,” he explained. “I just started building better habits little by little.”
The biggest surprise for Tom was not the physical change. It was the mental difference. He felt more confident, more organized, and more in control of his life.
One evening, while preparing dinner at home, he thought about the past year.
At the beginning, changing his routine felt almost impossible. But now, those small habits had become part of his normal life.
And for the first time in a long time, Tom felt balanced again.
questions: Why did Tom decide to change his routine?
What bad habits did he have before?
How did Lucas influence Tom?
Why did Tom focus on small changes?
What improvements did Tom notice after a few months?
Have you ever tried to change one of your habits?
answers_q: He often felt tired, stressed, and unhealthy.
He slept late, did not exercise, and ate unhealthy food.
Lucas explained how exercise improved his life.
He knew that big changes can be difficult to maintain.
He felt healthier, more energetic, and less stressed.
Example answer: Yes, I tried to sleep earlier and spend less time on my phone.
cloze: Tom often felt very ______ because of his routine.
He decided to change his ______ little by little.
At first, waking up earlier was very ______.
After some time, the new habits became part of his normal ______.
speaking: Talk about a habit you would like to change.
Why do you want to change it?
Is it difficult?
What small actions could help you?
How would your life improve?
example: I would like to improve my sleep routine because I often go to bed too late. Sometimes I feel tired during the day and cannot concentrate well. I think small changes could help me, like using my phone less at night and sleeping earlier. I believe I would feel healthier and more relaxed.
grammar: “Now, I feel healthier and more energetic.”

title: A Difficult Conversation with a Friend
content: Last week, Daniel had a difficult conversation with one of his closest friends, Lucas. They had known each other for almost ten years and usually understood each other very well. They often met after work, talked about their lives, and supported each other during difficult moments.
But recently, something had changed between them.

A few weeks earlier, Daniel organized a small dinner with several friends. Lucas promised he would come, but at the last minute, he canceled without much explanation. At first, Daniel tried not to think too much about it.
Later that evening, he noticed something on social media: Lucas had gone out with other people that same night.
Daniel stared at his phone for a few seconds.

After that, their conversations became colder and shorter. They still exchanged messages sometimes, but the atmosphere felt different. Lucas seemed distant, and Daniel became more frustrated each day.
Emma noticed the tension during lunch one afternoon.
“You and Lucas seem weird lately,” she said carefully.
Daniel explained the situation briefly. Emma listened quietly before responding.
“Maybe there’s more to the story,” she said.
“Sometimes avoiding the conversation creates even more tension.”
That sentence stayed in Daniel’s mind.

For several days, he continued thinking about the situation. Part of him wanted to ignore the problem completely. Another part knew that the friendship was too important to lose because of a misunderstanding.
Finally, on Friday evening, he sent Lucas a message.
“Can we meet tomorrow? I think we should talk.”
Lucas replied a few minutes later.
The next afternoon, they met in a quiet café near the city center. At first, the atmosphere felt uncomfortable. Both of them seemed slightly nervous.
“So… what did you want to talk about?”
Daniel hesitated for a moment before answering.
“I felt hurt after the dinner situation,” he admitted honestly. “You canceled at the last minute, and then I saw that you went out with other people instead.”
“Oh,” he said quietly. “I didn’t realize you saw that.”
For a few seconds, neither of them spoke.
Then Lucas leaned back in his chair and sighed.

“Honestly, things have been complicated lately,” he explained. “I’ve been stressed because of work and some personal problems. That night, I wasn’t feeling well mentally. Later, some friends invited me out for a short walk, and I accepted because I needed to clear my head.”
“At the time, I didn’t know how to explain all that,” Lucas continued. “So I just canceled quickly. I know it looked bad.”
“I think what upset me most was the lack of communication,” he admitted. “I felt like you didn’t care.”
“I do care,” he replied immediately. “I should have explained things better.”
The tension between them slowly started to disappear.
“To be honest, I also felt frustrated recently.”
“Because sometimes when you’re stressed, you disappear too,” Lucas explained calmly. “And I never really say anything about it.”
Daniel stayed silent for a moment because deep inside, he knew Lucas was right.
“Yeah… maybe I do that sometimes,” he admitted.

For the next hour, they talked more openly than they had in months. They did not agree on everything, but both of them tried to understand the other person’s perspective instead of simply defending themselves.
By the end of the conversation, the atmosphere felt lighter again.
As they left the café, Lucas smiled slightly.

While walking home, Daniel reflected on the situation. At first, he wanted to avoid the conversation because he was uncomfortable. But in reality, speaking honestly helped solve the problem much faster.
He realized something important that day:
Good communication does not mean avoiding conflict. Sometimes it means having difficult conversations with respect and honesty.
And for strong friendships, that effort is worth it.
questions: Why did Daniel feel upset with Lucas?
What advice did Emma give Daniel?
Why did Lucas cancel the dinner originally?
What was the real problem between them?
How did the conversation improve the situation?
Do you think honest communication is difficult? Why?
answers_q: Lucas canceled dinner and later went out with other people.
She advised him to talk directly with Lucas.
He was stressed and dealing with personal problems.
The main problem was poor communication.
They understood each other better and reduced the tension.
Example answer: Yes, because people are sometimes afraid of conflict or misunderstanding.
cloze: Daniel and Lucas had a serious ______.
Daniel felt ______ after the canceled dinner.
Emma said that avoiding conversations can create more ______.
Lucas explained that he was feeling very ______ lately.
In the end, both friends understood each other ______.
speaking: Talk about a misunderstanding you had with someone.
What happened?
How did you feel?
Did you talk about the problem?
What did you learn from the situation?
example: A few months ago, I had a misunderstanding with a friend because of messages. At first, I felt frustrated and did not want to talk about it. Later, we decided to meet and explain our feelings calmly. In the end, we understood each other better and improved our communication.
grammar: “I think what upset me most was the lack of communication.”
I understand your point of view.

title: Managing Time
content: In today’s world, managing time is becoming more and more important. Many people feel stressed because they constantly have too much to do. Between work, personal responsibilities, messages, meetings, and daily problems, it is easy to feel overwhelmed.
For a long time, Emma lived exactly like that.

Her days always felt chaotic. Every morning, she woke up late, rushed to work, skipped breakfast, and started answering emails immediately. During the day, she moved from one task to another without really finishing anything properly.
At first, she thought this was normal.
“I’m just busy,” she often told herself.
But little by little, the stress started affecting her more seriously.
One Tuesday morning, Emma arrived late to an important meeting with Daniel and Lucas. She forgot several documents and looked completely exhausted.
After the meeting, Lucas stopped her near the coffee machine.
“You seem stressed all the time lately.”
That evening, she returned home feeling mentally exhausted. She opened her laptop again to finish unfinished tasks, but after a few minutes, she realized she could not focus anymore.
Her apartment was disorganized. Her phone kept receiving notifications. She had started several projects but completed very few of them.
For the first time, she admitted something important to herself:
“I don’t manage my time well at all.”
The next day during lunch, Emma talked to Sophie about the situation.
“I feel overwhelmed constantly,” Emma admitted. “No matter how much I work, there’s always more to do.”
“I used to feel the same way.”
“Yeah,” Sophie replied. “What helped me most was becoming more organized.”
Sophie explained that she had started using a simple schedule every day. Instead of trying to do everything at once, she focused on priorities.
“At first, it felt strange,” Sophie admitted. “But it reduced my stress a lot.”
That conversation motivated Emma to try something new.
The following Sunday evening, she sat at her kitchen table with a notebook and began planning her week. She wrote down her important tasks, meetings, appointments, and personal goals.
At first, the process felt complicated.
“How do people organize everything like this?” she thought.
She also started dividing tasks into smaller steps. Instead of writing “Finish project,” she created simpler objectives like:
That made the workload feel less intimidating.

During the first week, Emma still struggled sometimes. She underestimated how long tasks would take and occasionally forgot parts of her schedule. But slowly, she began understanding her own habits better.
One afternoon, Daniel noticed a difference during a team meeting.
“You seem more relaxed lately,” he commented.
“I’m trying to manage my time better.”
That comment made her feel more confident.
Emma also learned something important about priorities. Before, she tried to answer every message immediately and accept every request. As a result, she constantly felt pressured.
Now, she started asking herself simple questions:
Those small changes had a big impact on her daily life.
She became more organized, slept better, and even had more free time in the evenings. Instead of constantly feeling stressed, she felt more balanced and in control.
One Friday after work, Emma met Lucas for coffee.
“You seem different these days,” Lucas said.
“In a good way or a bad way?” Emma asked jokingly.
“I finally realized something,” she said. “Being busy all the time doesn’t mean being productive.”
“That actually makes a lot of sense.”

While walking home later that evening, Emma reflected on the past few months. Time management did not solve every problem in her life, but it helped her think more clearly and reduce unnecessary stress.
And for the first time in a long time, she no longer felt controlled by her schedule.
Instead, she felt in control of her life again.
questions: Why did Emma often feel stressed?
What problems did her poor organization create?
What advice did Sophie give Emma?
How did Emma start improving her time management?
What changes did Emma notice after a few weeks?
What helps you stay organized in daily life?
answers_q: She had too many tasks and did not manage her time well.
She felt overwhelmed, tired, and disorganized.
Sophie advised her to use a schedule and focus on priorities.
She started planning her week and organizing tasks into smaller steps.
She felt less stressed and more balanced.
Example answer: Writing lists and planning my day help me stay organized.
cloze: Emma often felt ______ because she had too much to do.
Emma learned to focus on her ______.
Little by little, she became more ______.
Time management helped Emma feel more in ______ of her life.
speaking: Talk about how you organize your time.
Do you use a schedule?
What tasks are difficult to manage?
What helps you stay focused?
Would you like to improve your routine?
example: I try to organize my time by writing a list of important tasks every morning. Sometimes I still feel stressed when I have too much work, but planning helps me stay focused. I also try to avoid spending too much time on my phone while working. Little by little, I am becoming more organized.

title: A Challenge at Work
content: Recently, Lucas faced one of the biggest challenges of his professional life. His company had just received an important request from a new client, and the deadline was extremely short.
On Monday morning, Daniel called Lucas into a meeting room.
“We have a problem,” Daniel said seriously. “The client needs the full project by Friday.”
“I know,” Daniel replied. “But this project is very important for the company.”

Daniel explained the situation quickly. The client wanted a complete presentation, financial analysis, and several recommendations before the end of the week. Normally, a project like this would take at least two weeks.
At first, Lucas felt completely overwhelmed.
As he returned to his desk, he stared silently at the long list of tasks in front of him.
The pressure immediately started affecting him. His inbox was full of messages, meetings kept being added to his calendar, and everyone seemed stressed.
Emma noticed his expression during lunch.
“I just received a huge project with almost no time to finish it.”
“It is,” Lucas admitted. “Honestly, I don’t even know where to start.”
Emma stayed quiet for a moment before answering.
“Then maybe don’t think about the entire project at once.”
“Focus on one step at a time,” she explained. “Big tasks always feel scary at the beginning.”
That advice helped him calm down slightly.
Back at his desk, Lucas opened a notebook and decided to organize everything more clearly. Instead of thinking about the project as one enormous problem, he divided it into smaller tasks.
Suddenly, the project looked more manageable.
The next morning, Lucas arrived early at the office and started working step by step. Instead of panicking, he focused on completing one task at a time.
On Wednesday afternoon, he realized he was behind schedule. One part of the financial analysis was taking much longer than expected. For a few minutes, he felt the stress returning strongly.
“I’m never going to finish this on time,” he whispered.
At first, he considered staying silent and trying to solve everything alone. But then he remembered an important lesson from previous experiences: asking for help is not a weakness.
So he walked over to Sophie’s desk.
“Do you have a few minutes?” he asked.
Lucas explained the problem carefully. Sophie listened and immediately offered practical advice. She even helped him simplify part of the analysis.
“That already saves me so much time,” Lucas admitted.
“That’s what teamwork is for,” Sophie answered with a smile.
That conversation gave Lucas more confidence.
During the following two days, he stayed focused and organized. He avoided unnecessary meetings, limited distractions, and continued following his schedule carefully.
Little by little, the project started coming together.
On Friday afternoon, Lucas finally sent the completed presentation to Daniel for final review.
A few minutes later, Daniel walked to his desk.
Then he looked up and smiled slightly.
“Honestly,” Daniel continued, “I know the timeline was difficult. You handled the pressure well.”
For the first time all week, Lucas finally relaxed.
Later that evening, the team went for coffee together after work. Emma smiled while looking at him.

While walking home afterward, Lucas reflected on the experience. At the beginning, the project seemed too large and stressful to manage. But staying calm, organizing the work carefully, and asking for support made a huge difference.
When people face difficult challenges, the solution is not panic. The solution is structure, communication, and patience.
And sometimes, progress happens one small step at a time.
questions: Why was the project difficult for Lucas?
How did Emma help Lucas?
Why did Lucas divide the project into smaller tasks?
What problem happened on Wednesday?
How did Sophie help Lucas?
What lesson did Lucas learn from the experience?
answers_q: The deadline was very short and the project was large.
She advised him to focus on one step at a time.
It helped the project feel more manageable.
He realized he was behind schedule.
She gave advice and helped simplify part of the analysis.
He learned the importance of organization, communication, and patience.
cloze: Lucas felt very ______ when he saw the deadline.
Emma advised him to work step by ______.
Lucas divided the project into smaller ______.
Sophie helped Lucas solve part of the ______.
In the end, Lucas completed the project ______.
speaking: Talk about a difficult challenge you faced.
What was the situation?
Why was it stressful?
Did someone help you?
What did you learn from the experience?
example: Last year, I had to prepare an important exam in a very short time. At first, I felt overwhelmed because there was too much information to study. Then, I organized my work into smaller sections and created a schedule. I also asked classmates for advice when I needed help. In the end, I passed the exam and felt very proud.
grammar: “I divided the work into smaller tasks.”
“This experience taught me to stay organized.”

title: Living Alone
content: When Anna decided to move into her own apartment last year, she felt both excited and nervous. For several years, she had lived with roommates in a busy apartment near the city center. There was always noise, conversations, and activity around her.
But at the same time, she was never truly alone.

After receiving a promotion at work, Anna finally had enough money to rent a small apartment by herself. It was not very large, but it felt comfortable and peaceful. The apartment had big windows, a tiny kitchen, and a small balcony where she could drink coffee in the morning.
“This is exactly what I wanted,” she told Emma while showing her the apartment for the first time.
“It looks really nice. You finally have your own space.”
At first, living alone felt exciting.
Anna could organize everything exactly the way she wanted. She chose her own schedule, decorated the apartment herself, and enjoyed the silence after long days at work.
For the first few weeks, she loved the independence.
Nobody used her food. Nobody made noise late at night. Nobody left dishes in the sink.
But after some time, things started feeling different.
One evening after work, Anna entered the apartment and immediately noticed how quiet everything was. She placed her bag on the floor, looked around the empty room, and suddenly felt strange.
Usually, someone was there to say hello or ask about her day.
At first, she tried to ignore the feeling. She turned on music while cooking dinner and spent more time watching series online. But little by little, loneliness became more noticeable.
One Friday evening, Lucas called her.
“Do you want to come out with us tonight?” he asked.
“Honestly… I was thinking about staying home.”
“You’ve been saying that a lot recently.”
Living alone had made her more independent, but it also became easier to isolate herself without noticing.
The following weekend, Emma visited again. While drinking coffee on the balcony, Emma asked an important question.
“So… how do you really feel about living alone?”
“I like the freedom,” she admitted. “I feel more organized and more responsible now.”
“That’s normal. Independence and loneliness can exist at the same time.”
That sentence stayed in Anna’s mind for days.
After that conversation, she decided to create a better balance in her life. She still enjoyed her independence, but she stopped isolating herself too much.
She invited friends over for dinner more often. Sometimes she worked from cafés instead of staying home all day. She also called her family regularly instead of only sending short messages.
Little by little, the apartment started feeling warmer and more alive.

Anna also discovered positive things about living alone that she had not noticed before. She became more confident and more comfortable making decisions independently. She learned how to manage bills, organize her time better, and solve small daily problems by herself.

One evening, while preparing dinner in her quiet kitchen, she reflected on the past year. At the beginning, living alone felt strange and slightly uncomfortable. Then it became difficult emotionally for a while.
She no longer feared the silence.
Instead, she understood how to balance solitude and social connection in a healthier way.
A few days later, Anna met Lucas and Emma for dinner. During the conversation, Lucas smiled and asked:
“So, do you still enjoy living alone?”
“It still happens sometimes,” she admitted. “But now I know how important it is to stay connected with people.”
Emma raised her coffee cup slightly.
Because living alone had not only changed her apartment.
It had changed the way she understood herself.
questions: Why did Anna decide to live alone?
What did she enjoy at first?
Why did she start feeling lonely?
What advice or support did Emma and Lucas give her?
What changes did Anna make to feel better?
Do you think living alone is easy or difficult? Why?
answers_q: She received a promotion and wanted her own space.
She enjoyed the freedom, silence, and independence.
There was no one to talk to when she came home.
They encouraged her to stay socially connected.
She spent more time with friends and family.
Example answer: I think it can be both positive and difficult because people need both freedom and connection.
cloze: Anna moved into her own ______ last year.
At first, she enjoyed the ______ of living alone.
Emma explained that independence and loneliness can exist at the same ______.
Anna learned to create a better ______ in her life.
speaking: Talk about living alone or sharing a home with others.
Would you like to live alone?
What are the advantages?
What are the disadvantages?
How important is social connection for you?
example: I think living alone can be both positive and challenging. On one hand, people have more freedom and independence. On the other hand, it can sometimes feel lonely. Personally, I enjoy having quiet time, but I also think spending time with friends and family is very important.
grammar: “Living alone can be both positive and difficult.”
“On one hand, you have more freedom.”
“On the other hand, it can feel lonely.”

title: A Bad Experience in a Restaurant
content: Last month, Emma had a bad experience in a restaurant while having dinner with her friends Lucas and Sophie. After a stressful week at work, they decided to relax and spend the evening together in a new restaurant that had recently opened near the city center.

The restaurant looked modern and comfortable. Soft music played in the background, the lights were warm, and the atmosphere felt calm. A waiter welcomed them politely and showed them to their table near the window.
“This place looks really nice,” Sophie said while opening the menu.
“Yeah,” Lucas agreed. “I heard the food here is excellent.”
“Honestly, I’m just happy to finally relax.”
At the beginning of the evening, everyone felt comfortable and excited. They ordered drinks, talked about work and daily life, and laughed together.
However, after some time, the atmosphere started changing.
Their food still had not arrived.
At first, they tried to stay patient.
“Maybe the kitchen is busy,” Lucas suggested.
Emma nodded, but she was starting to feel slightly annoyed. Around them, other tables were already receiving meals even though they arrived later.
Now the group was clearly frustrated.
Sophie checked her phone and sighed.
“We’ve been waiting for almost an hour.”
Emma looked around for the waiter, but he seemed overwhelmed and stressed. He moved quickly between tables without really stopping.
But after tasting her meal, her expression changed immediately.
“That’s honestly frustrating after waiting so long.”
For a moment, nobody knew what to do. Emma normally avoided complaining in restaurants because she disliked conflict. Part of her wanted to ignore the problem and simply finish dinner quietly.
But another part of her felt that the situation was unfair.
“You should probably say something,” he said calmly.
After a few minutes, she politely asked to speak with the manager.
A man in his forties arrived at the table shortly afterward.
“Good evening,” he said professionally. “I understand there’s a problem?”
Emma stayed calm and explained the situation carefully.
“We waited a very long time for the food,” she said. “And unfortunately, several dishes arrived cold.”
The manager listened attentively without interrupting her. That surprised Emma slightly because she expected a defensive reaction.
“I’m very sorry about this experience,” he replied sincerely. “Tonight has been unusually busy, but that is not an excuse.”
His calm attitude immediately reduced the tension at the table.
“I would like to replace the dishes immediately, and the desserts will be offered by the restaurant.”
Emma exchanged a quick look with her friends.
About fifteen minutes later, the new meals arrived. This time, the food was hot, fresh, and much better. The atmosphere at the table slowly became relaxed again.
Sophie smiled after tasting her dish.
“Okay,” she admitted, “this is actually really good now.”
By the end of the evening, everyone felt calmer. The situation had started badly, but the manager’s reaction helped improve the experience significantly.
While leaving the restaurant, Lucas looked at Emma.
“But you stayed polite,” Sophie added. “That made a difference.”
On her way home, Emma reflected on the evening. She realized something important: problems can happen anywhere, even in good restaurants or professional environments.
What really matters is how people communicate and react to the situation.
Being calm, respectful, and honest often leads to better solutions than anger or silence.
And sometimes, one difficult conversation can completely change the outcome of an experience.
questions: Why did Emma and her friends go to the restaurant?
What problems happened during the dinner?
Why did Emma hesitate to speak to the manager?
How did the manager react to the complaint?
How did the situation improve in the end?
What would you do in a similar situation?
answers_q: They wanted to relax after a stressful week.
The service was very slow, and the food arrived cold.
She disliked conflict and did not want to complain.
He apologized and offered a solution politely.
The restaurant replaced the food and offered desserts.
Example answer: I would politely explain the problem and ask for a solution.
cloze: The group waited a very long time for their ______.
When the food arrived, it was not ______.
Emma decided to speak with the ______.
The manager apologized for the bad ______.
In the end, the atmosphere became more ______.
speaking: Talk about a bad customer experience you had.
What happened?
How did you feel?
Did you complain or stay silent?
How was the situation resolved?
example: A few months ago, I had a bad experience in a shop because the service was very slow. At first, I stayed patient, but later I decided to speak politely with an employee. The employee apologized and helped solve the problem quickly. After that, the situation became much better.
grammar: “We waited a long time for our food.”
“The manager apologized and offered a solution.”
There seems to be a problem.

title: Learning from Mistakes
content: Everyone makes mistakes, but for a long time, Sophie believed mistakes were something dangerous. Whenever she failed at something, even a small situation, she felt embarrassed and disappointed in herself.
As a result, she started avoiding difficult situations whenever possible.
At work, she rarely shared new ideas during meetings because she was afraid of saying something wrong. During group discussions, she preferred listening quietly instead of participating actively.
But little by little, Sophie realized that her fear was limiting her confidence and slowing her progress.
One afternoon, her manager Daniel organized a team meeting about a new project. Everyone was encouraged to share suggestions and possible solutions.
Sophie actually had an interesting idea.
She had spent several days analyzing part of the project and noticed a possible improvement. Still, she stayed silent.
“What if my idea sounds stupid?” she thought.
A few minutes later, another colleague suggested almost the same idea.
After the meeting, Emma noticed her expression.
“I had the same idea earlier, but I didn’t say anything.”
“You know, nobody learns without making mistakes.”
Sophie nodded slightly, but deep inside, she still struggled with that idea.
A few weeks later, another situation changed her perspective completely.
Daniel asked Sophie to lead part of an important client presentation. At first, she wanted to refuse immediately.
“I’m not the best person for this,” she said nervously.
“What if I make mistakes during the presentation?”
“Sophie, everyone makes mistakes sometimes. What matters is how people react afterward.”
Even after that conversation, Sophie still felt stressed.
During the days before the presentation, she prepared carefully. She practiced several times, reviewed her notes repeatedly, and tried to anticipate possible questions.
Still, on the day of the meeting, she felt extremely nervous.
At the beginning of the presentation, everything went well. Sophie explained the project clearly and answered several questions confidently.
Then suddenly, she made a mistake while explaining some numbers on one slide.
For a second, her mind went completely blank.
But instead of pretending nothing happened, Sophie stopped calmly and corrected herself.
“Sorry,” she said honestly. “Those numbers are incorrect. Let me explain the updated figures.”
The client nodded politely, and the presentation continued normally.
To Sophie’s surprise, nobody reacted negatively.
After the meeting, Daniel spoke with her privately.
“You handled that situation very professionally,” he said.
“Yes,” Daniel replied calmly. “And then you corrected it. That’s normal.”
That conversation stayed in Sophie’s mind for a long time.
Later that evening, she met Lucas for coffee and explained what happened.
“I thought the presentation would be a disaster after my mistake,” she admitted.
“Honestly, people usually remember confidence more than perfection.”
Little by little, Sophie started changing the way she viewed mistakes. Instead of seeing them as proof of failure, she began treating them as opportunities to improve.
Now, when she makes mistakes, she tries to analyze the situation calmly.
What can I improve next time?
That approach helped her grow professionally and personally.
Of course, mistakes still feel uncomfortable sometimes. Nobody enjoys failing or making errors in front of other people.
But Sophie no longer avoids difficult situations because of fear.
Instead, she understands something important now:
Progress does not come from avoiding mistakes.
It comes from learning how to face them, understand them, and improve afterward.
And in many ways, that realization changed her confidence completely.
questions: Why was Sophie afraid of making mistakes?
What happened during the team meeting?
Why was Sophie nervous about the client presentation?
What mistake did Sophie make during the presentation?
How did Daniel react afterward?
What important lesson did Sophie learn?
answers_q: She was afraid of looking wrong or failing.
She stayed silent even though she had a good idea.
She feared making mistakes in front of the client.
She explained incorrect numbers on one slide.
He said she handled the situation professionally.
She learned that mistakes are part of learning and growth.
cloze: Sophie often avoided difficult ______ because of fear.
During the meeting, she stayed ______ instead of sharing her idea.
Sophie felt very ______ before the client presentation.
After the mistake, Sophie calmly ______ herself.
speaking: Talk about a mistake that helped you learn something.
What happened?
How did you feel at first?
What did you learn from the experience?
Did it help you improve later?
example: Last year, I made a mistake during an important exam because I did not manage my time well. At first, I felt disappointed and frustrated. However, after analyzing the situation, I understood what I needed to improve. Since then, I have become more organized and more confident during exams.

title: Working in a Team
content: Working in a team can sometimes be difficult, especially when people have different personalities, opinions, and ways of working. In theory, teamwork sounds simple. But in reality, communication problems, misunderstandings, and stress can quickly create tension.
Lucas learned this lesson during an important project at work a few months ago.
His company was preparing a presentation for a new client, and Daniel created a small project team to work on it. The group included Lucas, Emma, Sophie, and another colleague named Nathan.
“This project could be very important for the company,” Daniel explained during the first meeting. “I need all of you to work closely together.”
However, problems started appearing almost immediately.

During the first brainstorming session, everyone had different ideas about the project. Emma wanted a modern and creative presentation. Nathan preferred something more technical and detailed. Sophie focused mainly on organization and deadlines.
Lucas quickly realized that nobody fully agreed with each other.
At first, the discussions stayed polite.
But after a few days, the atmosphere became more tense.
One afternoon, Emma looked frustrated during a meeting.
“We keep discussing the same thing again and again,” she said. “We’re losing time.”
“I just think we need more precise information before making decisions.”
“But if we overanalyze everything, we’ll never move forward.”
The room became silent for a few seconds.
Lucas felt uncomfortable. He understood both perspectives, but the team was clearly struggling to work efficiently together.
Later that day, Sophie spoke privately with Lucas near the coffee machine.
“This project is becoming stressful,” she admitted.
“I think the real problem is communication.”
At first, Lucas was not completely convinced. He thought the issue was simply about different personalities.
But during the following meetings, he started noticing something important: people were listening only to respond, not to truly understand each other.
Everyone wanted their own ideas accepted immediately.
A few days later, Daniel organized another meeting to review the project’s progress. The atmosphere was still tense, and the discussion quickly became complicated again.
Finally, Daniel interrupted the conversation calmly.
“Okay,” he said. “Let’s stop for a second.”
“We’re acting like we’re competing against each other,” Daniel continued. “But this is supposed to be teamwork.”
Then Daniel added something simple but important:
“Before responding to someone’s idea, try to understand it completely first.”
That comment changed the atmosphere slightly.
During the next part of the meeting, Lucas decided to approach things differently. Instead of defending his own opinions immediately, he started asking more questions.
“Nathan,” he asked calmly, “what information do you think is missing exactly?”
Nathan explained his concerns more clearly this time. Surprisingly, his ideas actually made sense when people listened carefully.
Then Emma explained why she wanted a simpler presentation style.
“The client already receives too much technical information every day,” she said. “We need something clear and engaging.”
Little by little, the conversation became more constructive.
Instead of arguing, the team started combining ideas together.
Sophie organized the tasks carefully, Emma focused on presentation design, Nathan improved the technical analysis, and Lucas coordinated communication between everyone.
The project finally started moving forward smoothly.
A week later, the team presented the final result to Daniel.
After reviewing the presentation carefully, he smiled.
“This is strong work,” he said. “You improved a lot as a team.”
Later that evening, the group went for coffee after work. The atmosphere felt completely different from the stressful meetings at the beginning.
“Honestly, I thought we would never survive this project.”
Lucas reflected on the experience for a moment.
At the beginning, the team focused too much on individual opinions. But once people started listening carefully and communicating respectfully, everything improved.
He realized something important that day:
Good teamwork is not about everyone thinking the same way.
It is about understanding differences, communicating clearly, and working toward the same goal together.
And sometimes, listening carefully is more powerful than speaking first.
questions: Why was teamwork difficult for Lucas’s group at first?
What caused tension during the meetings?
What important advice did Daniel give the team?
How did Lucas change his approach during discussions?
Why did the project improve later?
What qualities are important in good teamwork?
answers_q: Everyone had different ideas and communication problems.
People focused too much on defending their own opinions.
He told them to understand ideas before responding.
He started asking questions and listening more carefully.
The team communicated better and combined their strengths.
Example answer: Listening, communication, patience, and respect are important.
cloze: The project team had different ______ about the work.
At first, the meetings became very ______.
Daniel explained that teamwork requires good ______.
Lucas started asking more ______ during discussions.
In the end, the team worked together more ______.
speaking: Talk about an experience working in a team.
Was teamwork easy or difficult?
Did people have different opinions?
How did the group solve problems?
What makes a team successful?
example: Last year, I worked on a group project at school. At first, communication was difficult because everyone had different ideas. Sometimes we disagreed about organization and deadlines. However, after several discussions, we learned to listen better and divide the work clearly. In the end, the project was successful because we worked together more effectively.
grammar: I understand your point of view.

title: A Personal Goal
content: At the beginning of this year, Emma decided to focus seriously on her health. For a long time, she had ignored small warning signs because she was busy with work and daily responsibilities. She often felt tired, stressed, and unmotivated, but she kept telling herself that things would improve later.

Her routine had slowly become unhealthy. She slept badly, exercised very little, and often ate fast food because it was quick and convenient. After long workdays, she usually stayed on the sofa watching series instead of taking care of herself.
One Monday morning, Emma climbed the stairs to her office and immediately felt exhausted.
“Honestly? I really need to improve my health.”
“I’ve been trying to improve mine too. It takes time.”
That conversation stayed in Emma’s mind all day.
Later that evening, while looking at herself tiredly in the bathroom mirror, she finally admitted something important:
“If I don’t change my habits now, nothing will improve.”
At first, she felt overwhelmed because she did not know where to start. Every time she searched online, she found complicated advice, extreme diets, or unrealistic fitness programs.
Then Sophie gave her a simple piece of advice during lunch one day.
“Don’t try to change your entire life immediately,” Sophie explained. “Start with small, realistic habits.”
That idea made much more sense to Emma.
So instead of creating impossible goals, she made a simple plan.
First, she decided to exercise three times a week. Nothing extreme. Just regular movement. She started with short walks after work and simple workouts at home.
After only fifteen minutes of exercise, Emma already felt tired.
“This is harder than I expected,” she admitted to Lucas one evening.
“At the beginning, everybody feels that way.”
Emma also started improving her diet little by little. She did not follow a strict diet because she knew it would frustrate her quickly. Instead, she focused on simple changes:
Other days were much more difficult.
There were evenings when Emma wanted to give up completely. After stressful workdays, ordering fast food and skipping exercise felt easier.
One rainy Thursday evening, she almost canceled her workout again.
“I’ll restart next week,” she thought while sitting on her sofa.
But then she remembered something Daniel said during a discussion at work:
Emma realized she did not need to be perfect every day. She simply needed to continue.
So she put on her shoes and completed a short workout anyway.
Little by little, her new habits became easier.
After a few months, Emma started noticing small but important changes. She slept better, had more energy during the day, and felt less stressed overall. Even her concentration at work improved.
One afternoon, Sophie smiled while looking at her.
The physical changes were encouraging, but the biggest difference was mental. Emma felt proud of herself for staying consistent. For the first time in a long time, she trusted herself more.
One weekend morning, while preparing breakfast after an early workout, she reflected on her progress.
At the beginning of the year, her goal felt difficult and far away. Now, she realized that real progress came from small actions repeated regularly.
She still had goals to reach, of course.
But now she believed something important:
Improving your life does not happen overnight.
It happens one decision, one habit, and one day at a time.
And sometimes, becoming healthier also helps you become more confident in every other part of life.
questions: Why did Emma decide to improve her health?
Why did she feel overwhelmed at first?
What advice did Sophie give her?
What changes did Emma make in her routine?
Why was consistency important for Emma?
Have you ever set a personal health goal?
answers_q: She often felt tired, stressed, and unhealthy.
She found too much complicated advice online.
Sophie advised her to start with small, realistic habits.
She exercised regularly and improved her diet.
It helped her continue even on difficult days.
Example answer: Yes, I wanted to sleep better and exercise more regularly.
cloze: Emma wanted to improve her ______ this year.
At first, she did not know where to ______.
She started exercising three times a ______.
Some days, Emma wanted to give ______.
Little by little, she became more ______.
speaking: Talk about a personal goal you would like to achieve.
What is your goal?
Why is it important?
What difficulties could you face?
How can you stay motivated?
example: One of my personal goals is to improve my physical condition. I would like to exercise more regularly and develop healthier habits. I know it will take time, especially because motivation changes from day to day. However, I think consistency and small daily actions can help me succeed.
grammar: “I started exercising three times a week.”
“Now, I feel stronger and more confident.”

title: Technology in Daily Life
content: Technology plays an important role in modern life. Today, most people use phones, computers, and online services every day without even thinking about it. Technology helps people work faster, communicate easily, and access information almost instantly.
For Emma, technology had always been part of her daily routine.

Every morning, she checked her phone before getting out of bed. Then she answered messages during breakfast, listened to podcasts while traveling to work, and spent most of the day in front of a computer.
At first, she never questioned those habits.
“Everybody lives like this now,” she often thought.

And in many ways, technology really helped her life. She could organize meetings online, communicate with friends quickly, and learn new things easily. When she wanted information, the answer was usually available in seconds.
One evening after work, Emma met Lucas and Sophie in a café.
During the conversation, Lucas suddenly laughed.
“You know what’s funny?” he said. “We’ve all been sitting together for ten minutes, and everybody checked their phone at least three times.”
Emma looked down at her phone immediately and smiled awkwardly.
At first, the conversation felt light and funny. But later that evening, Emma started thinking seriously about it.
The next morning, she noticed something strange during breakfast. She opened social media automatically without even deciding to do it consciously.
Suddenly, almost thirty minutes had passed.
“That’s actually a little scary,” she thought.
Little by little, Emma started realizing how dependent she had become on technology. Even during quiet moments, she felt uncomfortable without her phone nearby.
One weekend, she visited her parents for dinner. During the meal, her father looked slightly annoyed.
“You spend a lot of time on your phone lately,” he commented.
“I understand. But sometimes you’re physically here and mentally somewhere else.”
That sentence stayed in her mind afterward.
A few days later, Emma noticed another problem at work. During meetings, people often stopped listening fully because notifications interrupted conversations constantly.
One afternoon, Daniel became frustrated during a team discussion.
“Can we please focus for five minutes without checking our phones?” he asked seriously.
Emma realized that technology was helping people communicate more easily, but sometimes it also reduced real attention and connection.
Still, she did not believe technology itself was the problem.
In her opinion, the real issue was balance.
Technology could be extremely useful when used wisely. It allowed people to learn languages, work remotely, stay connected with family, and discover opportunities that were impossible years ago.
For example, Lucas recently started an online course to improve his professional skills. Sophie regularly used fitness applications to organize her workouts and health goals.
But Emma also understood that people needed limits.
So she decided to make small changes in her routine.
She stopped checking her phone immediately after waking up. She also started leaving her phone in another room while eating dinner or spending time with friends.
At first, those habits felt uncomfortable.
Sometimes she even wanted to check notifications automatically.
But little by little, she noticed positive changes. She felt calmer, more focused, and more present during conversations.
One evening, while walking home without headphones or social media, Emma suddenly realized something surprising:
For the first time in a long time, her mind felt quiet.
The next weekend, she met Lucas again for coffee.
“You seem more relaxed lately,” Lucas said.
“I still use it every day,” she explained. “But now I try to control it instead of letting it control me.”
“That actually makes a lot of sense.”
While returning home later that evening, Emma reflected on how much technology had changed modern life. It could improve communication, learning, and organization enormously.
But she also understood something important:
Good technology is not about using more tools all the time.
It is about using them consciously, with balance and intention.
Because technology should support life — not replace it.
questions: How does technology help people today?
Why did Emma start questioning her habits?
What comment did Emma’s father make during dinner?
Why did Daniel become frustrated during the meeting?
What changes did Emma make in her daily routine?
Do you think people are too dependent on technology today?
answers_q: It helps people communicate and access information quickly.
She realized she was constantly checking her phone automatically.
He said she was physically present but mentally somewhere else.
People kept checking their phones during the discussion.
She reduced phone use during meals and personal moments.
Example answer: Sometimes yes, because many people use phones constantly.
cloze: Technology plays an important ______ in modern life.
Emma realized she had become too ______ on her phone.
Daniel wanted the team to stay more ______ during meetings.
Balance helped Emma feel calmer and more ______.
speaking: Talk about technology in your daily life.
How often do you use technology?
What advantages does it give you?
Are there disadvantages?
How can people use technology more wisely?
example: Technology is very important in my daily life because I use it for work, communication, and entertainment. It helps me save time and access information quickly. However, sometimes I spend too much time on my phone or computer. I think balance is important because technology should help people, not control them.

title: A Difficult Choice
content: Sometimes, life requires difficult choices, especially when both options seem important for different reasons. A few months ago, Lucas faced exactly this kind of situation.

At the time, he was finally feeling more stable in his professional life. After changing jobs the previous year, he had become more confident and comfortable in his new company. His salary was good, his routine was organized, and his manager trusted him.
From the outside, everything looked positive.
But one evening, something unexpected happened.
Lucas received a message from an old university friend named Nathan. They had not spoken seriously in several years.
The next evening, they met in a small café after work. Nathan looked excited.
“I’m starting a new business project,” he explained. “And honestly, I think you’d be perfect for it.”

Lucas listened carefully while Nathan described the idea. The project involved helping small companies improve their digital communication and organization. Nathan wanted Lucas to join the team from the beginning.
At first, the idea sounded exciting.
Lucas had always wanted more freedom and creative responsibility in his work. The project sounded dynamic, modern, and full of potential.
But there was also a serious problem.
If he accepted, Lucas would leave the stability of his current job. The salary would be uncertain at first, and there was no guarantee that the project would succeed.
That night, Lucas returned home with dozens of thoughts in his mind.
“This could completely change my life,” he thought.
For several days, he could not stop thinking about the decision. During meetings at work, he sometimes lost focus because his mind kept returning to Nathan’s proposal.
One afternoon, Emma noticed his distracted expression.
“You seem somewhere else today,” she said.
“Honestly… I have an important decision to make.”
During lunch, he explained the situation to her.
“So basically,” she summarized, “you have one safe option and one exciting option.”
Over the following days, Lucas started analyzing the situation seriously. He made lists of advantages and disadvantages. He thought about finances, stress, long-term goals, and personal motivation.
The safe option offered comfort and security.
The second option offered growth, challenge, and uncertainty.
One evening, Lucas spoke with Sophie on the phone.
“I think stability is important,” she admitted. “But motivation matters too.”
That sentence stayed in his mind afterward.
Deep inside, Lucas already knew something important: although his current job was comfortable, he no longer felt truly excited by it.
Meanwhile, every time he thought about Nathan’s project, he felt energy and curiosity.
The night before giving his final answer, Lucas barely slept. He walked around his apartment thinking about all possible outcomes.
“What if I regret leaving my job?”
“What if this is exactly the opportunity I need?”
The next morning, he sat quietly with a coffee and reflected on his personal goals. He realized something important:
But sometimes difficult choices create growth.
That afternoon, Lucas met Nathan again.
“Yeah,” Lucas replied. “Honestly, I’m scared. But I think this is the right decision for me.”
The first weeks were intense and stressful. Lucas had to adapt quickly, manage uncertainty, and work longer hours than before.
But despite the pressure, something felt different.
Every day brought new challenges, ideas, and opportunities to learn. Even difficult moments felt meaningful because he believed in what he was building.
One evening after work, Emma sent him a message:
“No. It’s difficult sometimes… but I finally feel excited about the future again.”
And for him, that feeling was worth the risk.
questions: What difficult choice did Lucas face?
Why was the second option risky?
What advice did Emma and Sophie give Lucas?
Why was the decision stressful for Lucas?
Why did Lucas finally choose the second option?
Have you ever had to choose between safety and opportunity?
answers_q: He had to choose between a stable job and a new business project.
The salary and future were uncertain.
They encouraged him to think about motivation and personal goals.
He was afraid of making the wrong decision.
He wanted more motivation, growth, and challenge.
Example answer: Yes, sometimes opportunities are exciting but also risky.
cloze: Lucas received a business ______ from Nathan.

example: A few years ago, I had to choose between staying in a comfortable situation or trying something new. At first, I was afraid because the new option seemed risky. However, I wanted to grow and learn new things. In the end, I accepted the challenge, and today I feel more confident and motivated.
grammar: “I had to choose between two options.”
“I felt it was right for me.”

title: Dealing with Stress
content: Stress has become a common problem in modern life. Many people feel constant pressure because of work, responsibilities, money, or personal problems. For a long time, Emma believed stress was simply something people had to accept.
But eventually, she realized her situation was becoming unhealthy.

A few months ago, her daily routine was extremely exhausting. She woke up tired, rushed through the morning, answered work messages constantly, and often stayed awake late thinking about unfinished tasks.
Even during weekends, she struggled to relax.
At first, Emma ignored the problem.
“I’m just busy,” she kept telling herself.
But little by little, the stress started affecting her physically and mentally. She became more anxious, less patient, and emotionally tired.
One afternoon at work, Sophie noticed something was wrong.
“You seem really stressed lately,” she said carefully.
“Honestly, I feel overwhelmed all the time.”
“And when was the last time you actually relaxed?”
Emma stayed silent for a few seconds because she honestly did not know.
That evening, she returned home feeling completely exhausted. Instead of cooking dinner or exercising, she sat on the sofa scrolling endlessly on her phone.
After almost an hour, she suddenly realized something:
Nothing she was doing was actually helping her feel better.
The next morning, Emma woke up with the same heavy feeling of stress and anxiety. While preparing coffee, she looked outside the window and noticed several people walking calmly in a nearby park.
For some reason, that simple image stayed in her mind.
Later that evening, instead of going directly home after work, she decided to take a short walk.
She was so used to moving quickly and thinking constantly that slowing down almost felt uncomfortable. During the first few minutes, her mind continued focusing on work problems and unfinished tasks.
But little by little, something changed.
The fresh air helped her relax slightly. She started paying attention to simple things around her: the trees, the evening light, people talking quietly nearby.
For the first time in days, her thoughts slowed down.
The following week, Emma continued walking regularly after work. Sometimes the walks lasted fifteen minutes. Sometimes longer.
Then she added other small habits.
She started organizing her schedule more carefully instead of trying to manage everything mentally. She reduced unnecessary notifications on her phone and created short breaks during busy workdays.
At first, the changes seemed small.
But after several weeks, Emma noticed a real difference.
She still experienced stress sometimes, especially during difficult projects or busy periods at work. However, the stress no longer controlled her in the same way.
One afternoon, Lucas noticed her calmer attitude during a stressful meeting.
“You seem much more relaxed these days,” he commented afterward.
Emma thought for a moment before answering.
“I realized I can’t remove all stress from life,” she explained. “But I can change how I react to it.”
“That actually makes a lot of sense.”

Over time, Emma also understood an important lesson: stress becomes worse when people ignore it completely. In the past, she tried to continue working harder without stopping. But now, she understood that rest, organization, and balance were necessary.
One Saturday morning, while walking peacefully through the park again, Emma reflected on how much her mindset had changed.
Before, she believed managing stress meant eliminating every difficult situation.
Stress is part of life sometimes.
But healthy habits, better organization, and small moments of calm can make difficult situations much easier to handle.
And even though stressful days still happened occasionally, Emma no longer felt powerless against them.
Instead, she finally felt more in control of herself and her daily life.
questions: Why did Emma feel stressed all the time?
What signs showed that stress was affecting her?
What activity helped Emma relax at first?
What other habits did Emma change?
What important realization did Emma have about stress?
What helps you manage stress in your daily life?
answers_q: She had too much pressure from work and responsibilities.
She felt tired, anxious, and emotionally exhausted.
Taking walks after work helped her relax.
She organized her schedule better and reduced phone distractions.
She realized she could change how she reacted to stress.
Example answer: Exercise, music, and good organization help me manage stress.
cloze: Emma often felt tired and ______ because of stress.

Emma started organizing her ______ more carefully.
In the end, she felt more in ______ of her life.
speaking: Talk about how you deal with stress.
What situations make you stressed?
What activities help you relax?
Do you organize your time well?
What advice would you give to stressed people?
example: When I feel stressed, I try to take a short break and organize my thoughts calmly. Walking, listening to music, and sleeping well help me relax. I also try not to do too many things at the same time. Good organization helps me feel more in control and less overwhelmed.
grammar: “I started doing simple activities like walking.”
“This helped me feel more in control.”
“Now, I know how to manage stress better.”

title: A Missed Opportunity
content: Last year, Daniel missed an important opportunity at work, and for a long time, he regretted his decision.
At the time, he was working in a stable position at a large company. His routine was comfortable, predictable, and relatively safe. He knew his responsibilities well, and his manager trusted him.
From the outside, his career looked successful.
But inside, Daniel sometimes felt stuck.
Every day followed the same pattern: meetings, reports, emails, and deadlines. The work was stable, but it no longer felt exciting.
One Monday morning, his director asked to speak with him privately.
“We’re starting a new international project,” the director explained. “We need someone organized and reliable to join the leadership team.”
The project sounded ambitious and modern. It involved working with international clients, traveling occasionally, and managing new responsibilities.
Then the director asked an unexpected question:
“Would you like to be part of it?”
The opportunity could help his career significantly. It would allow him to learn new skills, meet new people, and gain more visibility inside the company.
But almost immediately, doubts started appearing in his mind.
It would require longer hours, more pressure, and stepping far outside his comfort zone. Daniel worried about failing or losing the stability he already had.
“Can I think about it?” he asked.
For the next several days, Daniel could not stop thinking about the decision. Part of him wanted to accept immediately.
Another part felt afraid of change.
One evening after work, he discussed the situation with Emma while walking home together.
“It sounds like a great opportunity,” Emma said.
“Maybe,” Daniel replied uncertainly. “But it also sounds stressful.”
“Do you actually not want the project,” she asked, “or are you just afraid?”
That question made him uncomfortable because deep inside, he already knew the answer.
Still, fear influenced his decision more than motivation.
Daniel continued analyzing every possible risk:
What if the project became too difficult?
Eventually, he convinced himself that refusing the opportunity was the safer choice.
The following week, he informed his director politely.
“I appreciate the offer,” he explained, “but I think I should stay focused on my current responsibilities.”
The stress disappeared immediately, and his normal routine continued exactly as before.
But a few months later, things started changing.
The new project became very successful inside the company. Emma joined the team instead, and little by little, she received more responsibilities, visibility, and professional opportunities.
One afternoon, Daniel attended a presentation about the project’s results.
While listening to Emma speak confidently in front of the team, he suddenly felt a strong feeling of regret.
“That could have been me,” he thought quietly.
For the first time, he fully realized that fear had controlled his decision.
Later that evening, he met Lucas for coffee and talked honestly about the situation.
“I think I made a mistake,” Daniel admitted.
“Because I refused the opportunity before even trying,” Daniel explained. “Not because I truly didn’t want it… but because I was afraid.”
“That happens to a lot of people.”
“The worst part is that I already knew the project could help my career.”
After that conversation, Daniel reflected seriously on the lesson he had learned. He realized something important:
Sometimes people believe they are making rational decisions, but in reality, fear is making the decision for them.
Of course, taking risks does not always guarantee success. Some opportunities fail, and some choices become mistakes.
But Daniel understood that avoiding every risk also has consequences.
Since then, he has tried approaching opportunities differently. Instead of focusing only on possible failure, he also thinks about possible growth and learning.
A few weeks ago, when another challenging opportunity appeared at work, Daniel reacted differently this time.
But instead of saying no immediately, he asked himself a new question:
“What could happen if I actually try?”
And for the first time in a long time, that question felt more exciting than frightening.
questions: What opportunity was Daniel offered?
Why did Daniel refuse the project?
What question did Emma ask him?
Why did Daniel regret his decision later?
What important lesson did Daniel learn?
Have you ever missed an opportunity because of fear?
answers_q: He was offered a leadership role in a new international project.
He was afraid of change and failure.
She asked if he truly did not want the project or if he was just afraid.
The project became very successful and helped other people’s careers.
He learned that fear can control important decisions.
Example answer: Yes, sometimes fear prevents people from taking opportunities.
cloze: Daniel was offered a new ______ at work.
