example: A few years ago, I had the opportunity to study abroad, but I refused because I felt nervous about leaving my comfort zone. Later, I regretted the decision because it could have been an important experience. Since then, I try to be more open to new opportunities and challenges.
grammar: “Later, I realized it was a mistake.”

title: Working Under Pressure
content: Sometimes, working under pressure is unavoidable, especially in professional environments where deadlines are strict and expectations are high. Lucas understood this very well after experiencing one of the most intense weeks of his career.
Everything started on a Tuesday morning.
Lucas had just arrived at the office when Daniel called him into a meeting room.
“We have a situation,” Daniel explained seriously. “One of our clients changed their requirements at the last minute.”
“It means,” Daniel continued, “we need to completely update the presentation before Friday.”
“I know. That’s why we need to stay organized.”

As soon as the meeting ended, Lucas returned to his desk feeling stressed. His inbox was already full, his calendar contained multiple meetings, and now he had an urgent project with a very short deadline.
At first, the pressure felt overwhelming.
For several minutes, Lucas simply stared at his computer screen without knowing where to begin.
“There’s too much to do,” he thought.
Emma noticed his expression almost immediately.
He explained the situation quickly. Emma listened carefully before responding.
“Okay,” she said calmly. “First, stop thinking about the entire project at once.”
That comment helped Lucas slow down mentally.

Instead of focusing on everything at the same time, he opened a notebook and started organizing the work carefully. He divided the project into smaller sections and identified the most important priorities first.
Suddenly, the project looked slightly more manageable.

Over the next two days, Lucas worked under constant pressure. He attended meetings, answered urgent emails, and revised documents repeatedly. Sometimes the stress returned strongly, especially when unexpected problems appeared.
On Wednesday afternoon, one important file disappeared temporarily because of a technical issue.
For a moment, Lucas felt panic again.
“This week is a disaster,” he whispered.
Sophie overheard him and walked over calmly.
“Take a breath,” she said. “We’ll solve it.”
Together, they recovered the missing file and continued working.
That moment reminded Lucas of something important: pressure becomes more difficult when people try to manage everything alone.

So instead of isolating himself, he started communicating more with the team. Emma helped simplify some presentation slides, Sophie reviewed several documents, and Daniel clarified the client’s final expectations.
Little by little, the situation improved.
Despite the pressure, Lucas stayed focused on one task at a time. He stopped wasting energy worrying constantly about the final result and concentrated on small steps instead.
By Thursday evening, the project was almost complete.
Lucas felt exhausted but also proud.

The next morning, the team presented the updated project to the client during an online meeting. At first, Lucas still felt nervous, but the presentation went smoothly. The client seemed satisfied and even complimented the team’s work.
After the meeting ended, Daniel smiled.
Later that afternoon, Emma sat beside him in the office kitchen.
“So,” she asked jokingly, “did you survive the pressure?”
“At the beginning, I thought stress would make me fail,” he admitted. “But staying organized really changed everything.”
While walking home that evening, Lucas reflected on the experience. The pressure had been intense, and the week was mentally exhausting. But it also taught him something valuable:
Working under pressure is not about staying calm naturally all the time.
It is about learning how to organize yourself, focus step by step, and continue moving forward even when situations feel difficult.
And every time people succeed in those moments, they become a little more confident afterward.
questions: Why did Lucas feel stressed at the beginning of the week?
What advice did Emma give him?
How did Lucas organize the project?
What problem happened on Wednesday afternoon?
Why did communication help the team?
What lesson did Lucas learn from the experience?
answers_q: The deadline was very short and the workload was large.
She told him not to think about the whole project at once.
He divided the work into smaller tasks and priorities.
An important file disappeared temporarily.
Teamwork reduced pressure and solved problems faster.
He learned that organization and focus help manage pressure.
cloze: Lucas had to finish the project before the strict ______.
At first, the pressure felt very ______.

In the end, the successful project made Lucas more ______.
speaking: Talk about a time when you worked under pressure.
What was the situation?
Why was it stressful?
How did you stay focused?
What did you learn from the experience?
example: Last year, I had to prepare an important presentation in a very short time. At first, I felt stressed because there was too much work to do. Then, I organized my tasks carefully and focused on one step at a time. My team also helped me when I had difficulties. In the end, the presentation was successful, and I became more confident.
grammar: “I start with the most important ones.”

title: Learning a Language
content: Learning a language takes time, patience, and regular practice. Many people start feeling motivated and excited at the beginning, but after some time, they become frustrated because progress feels slower than expected.
Emma experienced exactly that while learning English.
At first, she was extremely motivated. She downloaded language applications, bought notebooks, watched videos online, and created ambitious study plans.
“This year, I’m finally going to become fluent,” she told Sophie confidently one evening.
During the first few weeks, Emma studied intensely almost every day. She learned vocabulary quickly and completed many grammar exercises.
At the beginning, the progress felt exciting.
But after a few months, things became more difficult.

Even though Emma continued studying, she still struggled during conversations. Sometimes she understood individual words but missed the meaning of entire sentences. Other times, she knew grammar rules perfectly but could not speak naturally.
One afternoon after an online English lesson, Emma closed her laptop feeling discouraged.
Later that evening, she called Lucas.
“I study a lot, but when I try speaking, I still make mistakes constantly.”
Lucas understood her frustration because he had experienced similar problems while learning Spanish years earlier.
“Language learning is strange,” he explained. “Sometimes you improve without noticing immediately.”
Emma stayed quiet for a moment.
“That’s the problem,” Lucas answered calmly. “Most people do.”
That conversation made Emma reflect seriously on her expectations.
She realized she was focusing too much on perfection and not enough on consistency.
At first, she studied in extreme ways: several hours one day, then almost nothing for the next few days. Eventually, that routine became exhausting.
So she decided to change her approach completely.
Instead of trying to study perfectly, she focused on regular practice.
Every day, she spent a little time using English naturally:
and practicing speaking aloud at home.
At first, speaking still felt uncomfortable.
Sometimes she stopped in the middle of sentences because she forgot words or pronunciation. Other times, she mixed grammar structures incorrectly.
One evening during an online conversation class, Emma became embarrassed after making several mistakes.
“I’m sorry,” she said nervously. “My English isn’t very good.”
“Making mistakes means you’re using the language,” he replied. “That’s how people improve.”
For the first time, she understood something important: mistakes were not proof of failure. They were proof that she was practicing actively.
Little by little, Emma became more confident.
She stopped translating every sentence mentally before speaking. She focused more on communication than perfection. Sometimes her English was not perfect, but people still understood her.
Several months later, Sophie noticed a clear difference during a conversation in English.
“You sound much more comfortable now,” she said.
“Yeah,” Sophie replied, “but now you keep speaking instead of stopping.”
Emma laughed softly because it was true.
The biggest improvement was not grammar or vocabulary.

One evening, while listening to an English podcast during a walk, Emma reflected on her progress. At the beginning, she wanted immediate results and perfect fluency. But now, she understood that language learning was a long process.
Real progress happens slowly, through repetition, patience, and daily practice.
And sometimes, small improvements become visible only after months of consistent effort.
Now, Emma still studies English regularly. Some days feel easier than others, and frustration still appears occasionally.
But she no longer fears mistakes like before.
Because now she understands something important:
Making mistakes is not the opposite of learning.
It is part of learning itself.
questions: Why did Emma feel frustrated while learning English?
What advice did Lucas give her?
Why did Emma change her study approach?
What helped Emma become more confident?
What important lesson did her teacher teach her?
What helps you improve when learning something new?
answers_q: She felt her progress was too slow and made many mistakes.
He explained that language learning takes time and consistency.
Her old routine was too intense and exhausting.
Regular practice and speaking more helped her confidence.
Mistakes are part of the learning process.
Example answer: Practicing regularly and staying patient help me improve.
cloze: Emma expected very ______ results at the beginning.
Lucas explained that consistency is very ______.
Emma started practicing English a little every ______.
Little by little, Emma became more ______.
speaking: Talk about learning a language or another skill.
Why did you start learning it?
What difficulties did you face?
How do you practice regularly?
What motivates you to continue?
example: I started learning English because I wanted to communicate more easily and improve my professional opportunities. At first, I felt frustrated because progress seemed slow, especially when speaking. However, I continued practicing a little every day. Over time, I became more confident and comfortable using the language.
grammar: “Learning a language takes time and effort.”
“Even if you make mistakes, you improve.”

title: A Good Habit
content: Developing good habits can change people’s lives slowly but deeply. At first, small habits may seem unimportant, but over time, they can improve confidence, knowledge, health, and daily routines.
Sophie realized this after starting a very simple habit: reading every day.

A year ago, reading was not really part of her life. She spent most evenings watching videos, scrolling on her phone, or answering work messages late at night. After stressful days at work, she usually felt too tired to concentrate on books.
Honestly, reading seemed boring to her.
One evening, while having dinner with Emma and Lucas, the conversation turned toward personal development and routines.
Lucas mentioned that he had recently finished an interesting book about productivity.
“You actually read regularly?” Sophie asked, surprised.
“I stopped waiting for the perfect moment,” he explained. “I just read a little every day.”
That sentence stayed in Sophie’s mind afterward.
A few days later, while walking through a bookstore after work, she decided to buy a simple novel. At first, she was not completely sure why.
“Maybe I’ll read a few pages,” she thought.
That evening, she sat on her sofa and opened the book. After ten minutes, she already felt distracted. She checked her phone several times and struggled to stay focused.
“This is harder than I expected,” she admitted to herself.
At the beginning, Sophie created a very simple goal: read for fifteen minutes every evening before sleeping.
After stressful workdays, she sometimes wanted to skip reading completely and spend the evening watching series instead. A few times, she even forgot her goal entirely.
One night, Emma called her while Sophie was holding the book in her hands.
“Trying to become someone who reads regularly.”
“That’s normal,” Emma replied. “New habits always feel uncomfortable at first.”
Instead of focusing on perfect discipline, she decided to focus on consistency. Even if she only read a few pages sometimes, she continued the habit daily.
Little by little, things started changing.

Reading became easier and more natural. Sophie noticed that she concentrated better and spent less time endlessly scrolling on her phone. She also discovered that reading helped her relax mentally after stressful days.
One evening, she became so absorbed in a story that she completely forgot about checking notifications for almost an hour.
For the first time in months, her mind felt calm and focused.

Over time, reading also helped Sophie learn many new things. She started reading different types of books: novels, personal development, psychology, and even articles in English to improve her language skills.
At work, she noticed another unexpected change.
During meetings, she expressed ideas more clearly and felt more confident speaking. She also became more patient and focused during difficult tasks.
One afternoon, Daniel commented on it during a discussion.
“You seem much more organized and thoughtful lately,” he said.
“I think reading is helping me more than I expected.”
Several months later, reading no longer felt like an effort.
It had become part of Sophie’s normal routine.
Now, every evening before sleeping, she automatically prepared tea, turned off unnecessary notifications, and spent time reading quietly.
One weekend afternoon, while sitting peacefully in a café with a book in her hands, Sophie reflected on how much this small habit had changed her life.
At first, fifteen minutes of reading seemed insignificant.
But over time, those small daily moments improved her concentration, knowledge, confidence, and mental balance.
People often underestimate the power of small habits because results appear slowly.
But consistent habits quietly change people little by little every single day.
And sometimes, one simple routine can influence many parts of life in positive ways.
questions: Why did Sophie decide to start reading regularly?
Why was reading difficult for her at first?
What advice did Lucas and Emma give her?
How did Sophie stay consistent with her habit?
What positive changes did reading create in her life?
What good habit would you like to develop?
answers_q: She wanted to improve herself and create a better routine.
She was distracted and not used to concentrating on books.
They explained that small daily habits are important.
She focused on reading a little every day.
She became calmer, more focused, and more confident.
Example answer: I would like to exercise more regularly and sleep earlier.
cloze: Sophie started reading for a few ______ every evening.
At first, she found it difficult to stay ______.
Emma explained that good habits take ______.
Little by little, reading became part of Sophie’s ______.
The habit helped Sophie feel more calm and ______.
speaking: Talk about a good habit you have or would like to develop.
Why is this habit important?

What benefits can this habit bring?
example: One good habit I would like to develop is exercising regularly. I think it would improve both my physical and mental health. At first, staying motivated could be difficult, especially after stressful days. However, I believe small and regular actions are more effective than extreme changes.
grammar: “After a few weeks, it became easier.”
“Now, it is part of my routine.”

title: A Team Conflict
content: Recently, there was a conflict in Lucas’s team at work. At first, the situation seemed small and unimportant, but little by little, the tension started affecting the entire project.
The team was working on an important presentation for a new client. Everyone had different responsibilities, and the deadline was approaching quickly.
At the beginning, things were progressing normally.

Emma was responsible for the visual design of the presentation, while Nathan focused on the technical analysis and detailed information. Sophie organized the schedule and communication between the team members.
For the first few days, the collaboration worked relatively well. But then disagreements started appearing between Emma and Nathan.
Emma wanted the presentation to be simple, clear, and visually attractive.
“We need more technical detail,” he insisted during one meeting. “The client expects precise analysis.”
“If we add too much information,” she replied, “the presentation will become confusing.”
“I think removing details would make the project look unprofessional.”
The atmosphere in the room became tense.
At first, Lucas believed the disagreement would disappear naturally after some time. But over the following days, the tension became worse.
Meetings became uncomfortable. Emma and Nathan interrupted each other constantly, and small discussions quickly turned into arguments.
One afternoon, Sophie quietly spoke with Lucas after a meeting.
“This conflict is starting to affect the entire team,” she admitted.
At first, Lucas was unsure. Part of him wanted to avoid the situation completely because conflict made him uncomfortable.
But he also understood something important:
Ignoring the problem would probably make things worse.
So the next morning, he suggested organizing a team discussion focused only on communication and solutions.
At first, Emma and Nathan looked slightly uncomfortable about the idea.
That afternoon, the team sat together in a small meeting room. The atmosphere felt serious but calmer than before.
“I think we all want the same thing,” he said. “We want the project to succeed. But right now, we’re struggling to work together.”
“Today, let’s try to understand each other instead of defending ourselves immediately.”
For several seconds, the room stayed silent.
“I feel like my ideas are ignored sometimes,” she admitted honestly. “I understand the importance of technical information, but I also think communication needs to stay clear.”
Nathan listened carefully this time before answering.
“I understand your point,” he said calmly. “But I worry that we’ll oversimplify important details.”
The tone of the conversation slowly started changing.
Instead of arguing, people began explaining their reasoning more openly.
“Honestly,” she admitted, “both of you are right in different ways.”
“The project needs both clarity and strong analysis. Maybe the real solution is balance.”
Little by little, the team started discussing practical compromises instead of focusing only on disagreements.
They decided to simplify some sections of the presentation while keeping detailed technical information in separate supporting documents.
Suddenly, the situation felt much more constructive.
Over the next few days, the atmosphere improved significantly. Emma and Nathan still had different opinions sometimes, but now they communicated more respectfully and listened more carefully.
As a result, the project became stronger than before.
When the final presentation was completed, Daniel reviewed it carefully and smiled.
“This is excellent work,” he said. “The balance between clarity and analysis is very strong.”
Later that evening, while drinking coffee together after work, Emma laughed softly.
“Honestly, I thought this project was becoming a disaster.”

Lucas reflected quietly on the experience. At first, the conflict seemed negative and stressful. But in reality, the disagreement forced the team to communicate better and think more carefully about the project.
Conflict itself is not always the problem.
The real problem is poor communication and refusal to listen.
When people communicate respectfully and stay open to compromise, even difficult situations can create growth, stronger teamwork, and better results.
And sometimes, disagreements help teams improve more than easy conversations ever could.
questions: Why did Emma and Nathan disagree?
How did the conflict affect the team?
Why did Lucas organize a discussion meeting?
What important advice did Lucas give the team?
What compromise did the team find?
What can people learn from conflicts?
answers_q: Emma wanted clarity, while Nathan wanted more technical detail.
Meetings became tense and communication became difficult.
He wanted the team to communicate better and solve the conflict.
He advised them to understand each other before defending themselves.
They balanced simple communication with detailed supporting documents.
Example answer: Conflicts can improve communication and teamwork.
cloze: There was a serious ______ inside the team.
Emma and Nathan strongly ______ about the project.
Lucas organized a group ______ to solve the problem.
The team learned to listen more ______.
In the end, the project actually ______ because of the conflict.
speaking: Talk about a conflict or disagreement you experienced.
What caused the problem?
How did people react?
Was communication difficult?
How was the situation resolved?
example: During a group project at school, two students disagreed about organization and deadlines. At first, the atmosphere became stressful because nobody wanted to compromise. Later, we discussed the situation calmly and listened to everyone’s opinion. In the end, we found a better solution together and improved the project.

title: A Difficult Journey
content: Last month, Sophie experienced one of the most exhausting journeys of her life. What was supposed to be a simple trip quickly became stressful, complicated, and much longer than expected.

Sophie needed to travel to another city for an important professional meeting organized by her company. The meeting was scheduled for the following morning, so she decided to leave the day before to avoid unnecessary stress.
At first, everything seemed perfectly organized.

She arrived at the train station early, bought coffee, checked her ticket, and found her seat without any problems. The weather was good, the station was calm, and Sophie finally felt relaxed after several stressful weeks at work.
“This trip might actually be enjoyable,” she thought while looking out the window.
The train departed on time, and the first part of the journey went smoothly. Sophie listened to music, answered a few emails, and even spent some time reading.
But after about two hours, the atmosphere inside the train suddenly changed.
“Due to a technical problem on the railway line, the train will experience a significant delay.”
Passengers immediately started reacting around her.
Some people sighed loudly. Others checked their phones nervously. A man sitting nearby looked frustrated immediately.
Sophie felt her stomach tighten slightly.
At first, she tried to stay optimistic.
“Maybe it won’t be too serious,” she thought.
Unfortunately, the delay became longer and longer.
After almost forty minutes, Sophie realized something important: she was going to miss her connection to the second train.
That second train was essential because it was the final part of her journey.
As soon as the train finally arrived at the station, Sophie quickly checked the departure board.
She closed her eyes for a second, feeling tired and frustrated.
Now she had to wait several hours for the next train.
At first, the situation felt extremely stressful. Sophie worried about arriving late, losing energy before the meeting, and wasting the entire evening inside the station.
She called Emma while searching for a place to sit.
“You sound exhausted already,” Emma said after hearing her voice.
“My connection left without me,” Sophie explained. “And now I’m stuck here for hours.”
“Honestly, this day is becoming a disaster.”
Emma stayed quiet for a moment before answering calmly.
“Well… getting angry won’t make the train arrive faster.”
Sophie laughed softly despite her frustration.
After the conversation, Sophie took a deep breath and tried to calm herself. She realized she had two choices:
spend the next few hours stressed and frustrated,
or accept the situation and make the best of it.
So she decided to slow down mentally.
She bought something to eat, found a quiet corner of the station, and opened her book. Later, she even walked outside for a few minutes to get fresh air.
Little by little, the frustration became easier to manage.

Around her, many passengers continued complaining loudly about delays and transportation problems. But Sophie noticed something interesting: the more people focused on anger, the more exhausted they seemed.
Eventually, the next train finally arrived late in the evening.
Sophie entered the train feeling physically tired but emotionally calmer than before.
Several hours later, she finally reached her destination.
When she arrived at the hotel, she immediately sat on the bed and exhaled deeply.
The next morning, despite the difficult journey, the meeting went well. Sophie was still slightly tired, but she managed the situation professionally.
Later that evening, while talking with Lucas about the trip, she reflected on the experience.
“At first, I thought the delays completely ruined my day,” she admitted.
“Now I think I learned something useful.”
She thought for a second before answering.
“Some situations are outside our control. Staying calm doesn’t solve everything… but it makes difficult moments much easier to handle.”
And after such a long and frustrating journey, Sophie knew he was right.
questions: Why was Sophie traveling to another city?
What problem happened during the journey?
Why did Sophie miss her connection?
What advice did Emma give her?
How did Sophie calm herself during the wait?
What lesson did Sophie learn from the experience?
answers_q: She had an important professional meeting.
Her train was delayed because of a technical problem.
The delay caused her to arrive too late for the second train.
Emma told her that anger would not improve the situation.
She ate, read, and took a short walk outside.
She learned that staying calm helps manage difficult situations better.
cloze: Because of the delay, she missed her ______.
In the end, Sophie finally reached her ______.
speaking: Talk about a difficult trip or travel experience.
Where were you going?
What problems happened?
How did you react?
What did you learn from the situation?
example: Last year, I had a difficult flight because of bad weather. The plane was delayed for several hours, and I missed another connection afterward. At first, I felt frustrated and stressed, but later I tried to stay calm and focus on practical solutions. In the end, I arrived safely, even if the journey was exhausting.
grammar: “I had to wait for the next train.”

title: Making a Plan
content: Planning is an important part of daily life, especially when responsibilities become more complex. Without a clear plan, it is easy to waste time, forget important tasks, and feel overwhelmed.
Emma learned this lesson during a particularly stressful period at work and in her personal life.
A few months ago, everything felt disorganized for her.

Her days were full of meetings, deadlines, messages, and unfinished tasks. At home, things were not much better. She forgot appointments, postponed important errands, and constantly felt like she was running behind schedule.
At first, Emma tried managing everything mentally.
“I’ll remember it later,” she often told herself.
But later usually became another forgotten task.
One Monday morning, she arrived late to an important meeting because she confused two different schedules. When she entered the room, Daniel looked surprised.
“We started fifteen minutes ago,” he said calmly.
Emma apologized immediately and sat down feeling embarrassed.

The rest of the day did not improve much. She forgot to send an important document, missed a phone call from Sophie, and stayed at the office much later than expected because several tasks remained unfinished.
That evening, while returning home exhausted, Emma realized something important:
“This situation is becoming impossible to manage.”
The next day during lunch, Lucas noticed her frustration.
“I feel completely disorganized,” Emma admitted. “No matter how hard I work, I’m always behind.”
“I used to feel like that too.”
“I started planning things more seriously,” he explained. “Not perfectly. Just more clearly.”
“I’ve tried planning before,” she said. “I usually stop after a few days.”
“That’s because most people create impossible plans.”
Later that evening, she decided to try again — but differently this time.
Instead of creating a complicated schedule, she started with something simple.
First, she defined her main goals for the week:
Then she divided larger responsibilities into smaller tasks.
Emma also realized another important problem: she constantly planned too many things in one day.
At first, she believed productivity meant filling every free minute with tasks.
But that approach only created stress and frustration.
So she started being more realistic.
She added short breaks between activities and accepted that not everything could be completed immediately.
Little by little, her routine became more organized.
Of course, problems still appeared sometimes.
One Thursday afternoon, an unexpected client meeting completely changed her schedule. In the past, that situation would have ruined her entire day emotionally.
Instead of panicking, she adapted her plan calmly and reorganized her priorities.
Several weeks later, Sophie noticed a visible difference.
“You seem much calmer lately,” she said while they were having coffee together.
“It means I stopped trying to control every second perfectly,” Emma explained. “Now I focus more on priorities and flexibility.”
Planning had not made life perfect or stress-free. Unexpected situations still happened regularly.
But now, Emma felt more focused, more balanced, and more in control of her time.
One evening, while reviewing her notebook before bed, she reflected on how much her mindset had changed.
At first, planning felt restrictive and complicated.
Now, it felt helpful and reassuring.
A good plan is not about controlling everything perfectly.
It is about creating structure, reducing unnecessary stress, and adapting calmly when life changes unexpectedly.
And sometimes, even simple organization can make daily life feel much easier.
questions: Why did Emma feel overwhelmed at the beginning?
What problems happened because of her poor organization?
What advice did Lucas give her?
Why did Emma start dividing tasks into smaller steps?
How did Emma react differently to unexpected problems later?
Why is flexibility important when making plans?
answers_q: She had too many responsibilities and no clear organization.
She forgot meetings, tasks, and important calls.
He advised her to create simpler and more realistic plans.
Smaller tasks felt less stressful and easier to manage.
She adapted calmly instead of panicking.
Example answer: Because unexpected situations happen regularly in life.
cloze: Emma often felt very ______ because of her schedule.
Lucas explained that good plans should stay ______.
Emma started dividing large tasks into smaller ______.
Little by little, Emma became more ______.
A good plan helped Emma reduce unnecessary ______.
speaking: Talk about how you organize your daily life.
Do you usually make plans?
What helps you stay organized?
What problems can happen without planning?
How do you adapt when plans change?
example: I usually organize my week by writing important tasks in a notebook or on my phone. Planning helps me stay focused and avoid forgetting responsibilities. However, I also try to stay flexible because unexpected situations can happen. Good organization helps me feel calmer and more productive.
grammar: “I usually start by defining my goals.”
“Sometimes, things do not go as planned.”

title: A Personal Experience
content: A few months ago, Emma decided to try something completely outside her comfort zone: she joined a public speaking class.

For years, speaking in front of people had made her extremely nervous. Even during simple meetings at work, she often avoided talking too much. When presentations were necessary, she prepared carefully but still felt anxious every time.
Her heart beat faster, her hands became cold, and sometimes she forgot simple words while speaking.
At first, Emma believed she simply was not “the type of person” who could speak confidently in public.
One afternoon at work, Daniel asked her to present a small project update during a team meeting.
The presentation itself was short and relatively simple.
Still, Emma felt stressed for hours before the meeting started.

When it was finally her turn to speak, she became so nervous that she talked too quickly and forgot part of her explanation. Nobody reacted negatively, but afterward, Emma felt frustrated and embarrassed.
Later that evening, she met Sophie for dinner and talked about the situation.
“I hate public speaking,” Emma admitted immediately.
“Really? I didn’t think you were that uncomfortable.”
Sophie thought for a moment before asking:
“Have you ever tried improving it seriously?”
At first, the idea sounded uncomfortable.
Joining a public speaking class meant voluntarily putting herself in stressful situations. Part of her wanted to avoid the idea completely.
But another part of her was tired of feeling afraid every time she needed to speak publicly.
A few days later, after thinking carefully about it, Emma registered for a weekly evening class.
The first session was difficult immediately.
As soon as the instructor asked participants to introduce themselves in front of the group, Emma felt panic rising inside her.
“There’s no way I can do this,” she thought.
When her turn arrived, she stood up slowly and spoke quietly for less than a minute. Her voice shook slightly, and she avoided eye contact most of the time.
Afterward, she sat down feeling disappointed.
In fact, most people in the room looked just as nervous as she felt.
That realization helped her relax slightly.
Over the following weeks, Emma continued attending the classes regularly. Each session included small exercises:
Sometimes Emma still became anxious before speaking exercises. A few presentations went badly, and she occasionally forgot words again.
But instead of giving up, she continued practicing.
The instructor also gave helpful feedback after every session.
One evening after a short presentation, he smiled and said:
“Yes,” he replied. “Your voice is calmer now, and you connect better with the audience.”

Little by little, Emma started noticing changes outside the class too. During work meetings, she participated more actively. She explained ideas more clearly and no longer panicked every time people looked at her while speaking.
One afternoon, Lucas noticed the difference immediately after a client presentation.
“You sounded really confident today,” he said.
“Honestly, I never thought I’d hear someone say that.”
The biggest change was not perfection.
Emma still felt nervous sometimes before important presentations.
But now, the fear no longer controlled her completely.
Several months later, during one class exercise, Emma stood confidently in front of the group and gave a short presentation without looking at her notes once.
It grows slowly through practice, repetition, mistakes, and experience.
And sometimes, the best way to overcome fear is not avoiding difficult situations…
but facing them little by little until they no longer feel impossible.
For Emma, joining that class became much more than a communication exercise.
It became proof that people can change more than they think.
questions: Why was public speaking difficult for Emma?
What happened during the first class?
Why did Emma continue attending the sessions?
What kind of exercises did the class include?
What improvements did Emma notice over time?
What helps people become more confident?
answers_q: She felt nervous and afraid while speaking in front of people.
She spoke very quietly and felt anxious.
She wanted to improve and overcome her fear.
The class included speaking exercises and communication practice.
She became calmer, clearer, and more confident.
Example answer: Practice, experience, and regular effort help people become confident.
cloze: The instructor gave helpful ______ after presentations.
Little by little, Emma became more ______.
speaking: Talk about something that made you nervous at first.
What was the situation?
Why did it feel difficult?
Did you improve over time?
What did you learn from the experience?
example: A few years ago, I was very nervous about speaking English in front of other people. I was afraid of making mistakes and sounding uncomfortable. However, I practiced regularly and slowly became more confident. Now, I still make mistakes sometimes, but I am no longer afraid to speak.
vocab: public speaking → prise de parole en public
grammar: “Now, I am not afraid to speak.”

title: Balancing Work and Life
content: Balancing work and personal life is not always easy, especially when professional responsibilities become overwhelming. For a long time, Lucas believed that working more automatically meant being more successful.
At first, he was proud of his intense routine.

He answered emails late at night, accepted extra projects regularly, and often worked during weekends. His manager appreciated his dedication, and colleagues frequently described him as reliable and hardworking.
But little by little, the situation started affecting him negatively.
Lucas slept badly, canceled personal plans frequently, and stopped making time for hobbies or relaxation. Even when he was physically at home, his mind remained focused on work.
One evening, Emma sent him a message:
“Are you still coming to dinner tonight?”
Lucas looked at the time nervously.
That had become a common situation recently.
A few days later, Lucas visited his parents for lunch. During the meal, his mother looked at him carefully.
“But it’s always busy,” she replied quietly.
That comment stayed in his mind afterward.
Later that evening, while checking work emails again before sleeping, Lucas suddenly realized something uncomfortable:
His entire life revolved around work.
At first, he tried ignoring the problem. He convinced himself that things would improve later after finishing current projects.

One Friday afternoon, the situation became impossible to ignore. Lucas was preparing an important report when he suddenly lost focus completely. He reread the same paragraph several times without understanding it.
He leaned back in his chair feeling mentally exhausted.
“When was the last time you actually rested properly?”
Lucas thought for several seconds before answering.
That conversation forced him to reflect seriously about his habits.
Over the following weekend, Lucas decided to change some things in his routine. Instead of creating extreme rules, he focused on simple limits.
First, he stopped checking professional emails late at night unless the situation was urgent.
Then he started protecting certain personal moments:
and time for hobbies he had abandoned months earlier.
At first, those changes felt uncomfortable.
Part of him worried constantly about missing something important at work.
One evening, while turning off his laptop earlier than usual, he thought:
But little by little, something surprising happened.
Instead of becoming less productive, Lucas actually became more focused and efficient during working hours. He slept better, concentrated more easily, and felt less emotionally exhausted.
Emma noticed the difference quickly during a meeting.
“You seem much calmer lately,” she commented.
He also started reconnecting with activities he genuinely enjoyed. He returned to photography, spent more time outdoors, and saw his friends more regularly instead of canceling plans constantly.
One Saturday morning, while walking peacefully through a park with his camera, he reflected on how different he felt compared to a few months earlier.
Before, he believed success required sacrificing personal well-being completely.
Now, he understood something more important:
People cannot perform well constantly without taking care of themselves too.
Several weeks later, Daniel spoke privately with Lucas after a project meeting.
“You seem more balanced recently,” Daniel observed.
“That’s because I finally realized I can’t work all the time.”
“That realization usually comes later for many people.”
While returning home that evening, Lucas thought carefully about everything that had changed.
Work was still important to him.
But now he understood that personal well-being mattered too.
Because a successful life is not only about productivity or professional results.
It is also about health, relationships, peace of mind, and having time to actually enjoy life outside of work.
And for the first time in a long time, Lucas finally felt balanced again.
questions: Why did Lucas feel exhausted?
How did work affect his personal life?
What comment did Lucas’s mother make?
What changes did Lucas make in his routine?
Why did Lucas become more productive later?
Why is balance important in life?
answers_q: He worked too much and rested very little.
He canceled plans and stopped making time for himself.
She said he always seemed busy and tired.
He set limits, rested more, and restarted hobbies.
Better rest improved his focus and energy.
Example answer: Balance helps people stay healthy and happy.
cloze: He often checked emails late at ______.
Sophie worried that Lucas was close to ______ out.
Little by little, Lucas created better ______ in his life.
Now, Lucas feels more relaxed and ______.
speaking: Talk about balancing work and personal life.
Do you think balance is difficult today?
How do people become too stressed?
What activities help people relax?
What would you like to improve in your own routine?
example: I think balancing work and personal life is very important but sometimes difficult. When people work too much, they can become stressed and exhausted. Personally, I try to rest, spend time with friends, and do activities I enjoy. I believe balance helps people stay healthier and happier in the long term.

title: Making Better Decisions
content: Making good decisions is not always easy, especially when people feel pressure, stress, or uncertainty. Sometimes there are too many options, and sometimes emotions influence choices more than logic.
Lucas learned this lesson after making several impulsive decisions in the past.

A few years ago, he often reacted quickly without thinking carefully about consequences. If he felt excited about something, he accepted immediately. If he felt frustrated, he responded emotionally. At the time, he believed fast decisions showed confidence.
But little by little, he realized that acting too quickly sometimes created unnecessary problems.
One example stayed in his memory very clearly.

Several months earlier, Lucas received an email offering him the opportunity to join a professional training program abroad for two weeks. The program looked interesting, but it also required a lot of preparation and would temporarily increase his workload.
At first, Lucas felt overwhelmed just reading the message.
“I already have too much work,” he thought.
Without really analyzing the situation, he refused the opportunity almost immediately.
A few days later, Emma asked him about it during lunch.
“So, are you excited about the training program?” she asked.
“I don’t know,” Lucas admitted. “I reacted too quickly.”
“I saw extra work and stress, so I immediately said no without really thinking about the long-term benefits.”
Emma stayed quiet for a moment.
“Now I think I made a mistake.”
That situation frustrated him because deep inside, he understood the opportunity could have helped his professional development.
After that experience, Lucas started reflecting more seriously on the way he made decisions.
Fast decisions are not always bad, but emotional decisions without reflection can create regret later.
A few weeks later, another important situation appeared.
Nathan invited Lucas to participate in a side project outside work. The project sounded interesting but also risky because it required time, effort, and commitment.
At first, Lucas felt the same instinctive reaction as before:
part of him wanted to refuse immediately to avoid stress,
another part wanted to accept immediately because the project sounded exciting.
But this time, he stopped himself.
Instead of answering immediately, Lucas took time to reflect carefully.
He wrote down advantages and disadvantages in a notebook. He thought about his goals, responsibilities, available time, and possible consequences.
He also decided to ask for advice from people he trusted.
One evening, he discussed the situation with Sophie.
“I think you already know the answer.”
“But honestly,” Sophie continued, “taking time before deciding is already progress for you.”
That comment made him smile because it was true.
Lucas also spoke with Daniel at work.
Daniel listened carefully before giving simple advice:
“A good decision usually requires both logic and self-awareness.”
Lucas reflected on that sentence for several days.
Eventually, after thinking calmly and gathering different perspectives, he made his decision. This time, the choice felt clearer because it was based on reflection instead of stress or excitement.
And surprisingly, even though the situation still involved uncertainty, Lucas felt more confident afterward.
A few months later, while discussing personal growth with Emma, he reflected on how much his mindset had changed.
“In the past,” he admitted, “I thought good decisions came from confidence and speed.”
“Now I think good decisions come from reflection, patience, and understanding consequences.”

Of course, no decision is perfect. Sometimes people still make mistakes even after careful reflection. But now, Lucas no longer wanted fear, stress, or impulsive emotions to control important choices.
Instead, he tried asking himself better questions:
What advice could help me see the situation differently?
Because he finally understood something important:
Good decisions are rarely about acting quickly.
Most of the time, they are about slowing down enough to think clearly before moving forward.
questions: Why did Lucas regret refusing the training program?
What mistake did Lucas often make in the past?
Why did Lucas react differently during the second opportunity?
What advice did Sophie and Daniel give him?
Why did Lucas feel more confident later?
Why is reflection important when making decisions?
answers_q: The program could have helped his professional development.
He made emotional and impulsive decisions too quickly.
He wanted to avoid repeating past mistakes.
They advised him to think carefully and understand consequences.
His decision was based on reflection and logic.
Example answer: Reflection helps people avoid emotional mistakes.
cloze: Lucas often made decisions too ______ in the past.
Later, he realized some choices created ______.
This time, Lucas decided to take more ______ before answering.
Sophie said that reflection was already ______ for him.
Lucas learned to think about long-term ______.
speaking: Talk about an important decision you made.
What was the situation?
Why was the decision difficult?
Did you ask for advice?
What did you learn afterward?
example: Last year, I had to make an important decision about my studies and future career. At first, I felt confused because I had several options. Instead of deciding quickly, I took time to think carefully and asked for advice from people I trusted. In the end, I felt more confident because my decision was thoughtful and realistic.
grammar: “It is better to think before acting.”

title: A Difficult Task
content: Last week, Sophie had to complete a difficult task at work, and at first, she honestly had no idea how to begin.

On Monday morning, Daniel sent her an email with a new assignment related to an important client project. The task involved reviewing a large amount of information, organizing several documents, and preparing a detailed summary before the end of the week.
“How difficult can it be?” she thought.
But after opening the files and reading the instructions carefully, her confidence disappeared quickly.

The information was complex, several parts were incomplete, and some explanations seemed unclear. Different documents contained contradictory details, and Sophie struggled to understand exactly what the client expected.
After almost an hour, she was still staring at her screen feeling confused.
“This makes no sense,” she whispered quietly.
The more she looked at the task, the more frustrated she became.
At first, she considered asking Daniel for help immediately. But part of her wanted to solve the problem independently.
So instead, she continued reading the documents repeatedly.
Unfortunately, that approach only increased her stress.
By lunchtime, Sophie already felt mentally exhausted.
Emma noticed her expression while they were preparing coffee.
“I have this huge task, but the instructions are so unclear.”
“For a moment, I thought maybe I was just not understanding correctly.”
“Sometimes the task itself is badly organized.”
That comment made Sophie feel slightly better.
After lunch, she returned to her desk and decided to change her approach completely. Instead of trying to understand the entire project at once, she focused on one small objective at a time.
First, she opened a notebook and wrote down the main parts of the task:
Suddenly, the situation felt slightly less overwhelming.
Sophie also realized another important thing:
She did not need to solve everything immediately.
So she started working step by step.
At first, progress felt slow. Sometimes she still became confused and needed to reread information several times. Other moments felt frustrating because new questions kept appearing.
On Tuesday afternoon, she almost lost patience again after discovering missing information inside one document.
Lucas overheard her while walking past her desk.
After she explained the situation, Lucas gave simple advice.
“Don’t focus on finishing everything immediately,” he said. “Just focus on the next step.”
That sentence sounded simple, but it helped Sophie calm down mentally again.
Over the following days, the task slowly became clearer. Each small step helped her understand the bigger picture more easily.

She also became more comfortable asking questions when necessary instead of wasting hours feeling confused alone. Daniel clarified several unclear sections, and Emma helped her organize part of the information visually.
Little by little, Sophie regained confidence.
By Thursday evening, most of the work was complete.
When she finally finished the final summary on Friday afternoon, she leaned back in her chair feeling relieved.
Later that day, Daniel reviewed the work carefully.
“This is very well organized,” he said. “Good job.”
“Honestly, at the beginning, I thought I would never finish it.”
While returning home that evening, Sophie reflected on the entire experience.
At first, confusion and frustration made the task feel impossible. But once she slowed down, organized the work into smaller steps, and stayed patient, everything became more manageable.
Difficult tasks often feel overwhelming because people focus too much on the final result instead of the process.
But when people stay calm, organize the problem carefully, and move forward step by step, even complicated situations become clearer over time.
And sometimes, patience is just as important as skill.
questions: Why did Sophie feel confused at the beginning?
What made the task frustrating?
What advice did Emma and Lucas give her?
Why did Sophie create smaller steps?
How did the task become easier over time?
What lesson did Sophie learn from the experience?
answers_q: The instructions and documents were unclear.
Some information was contradictory or missing.
They advised her to stay calm and focus step by step.
Smaller steps made the task feel more manageable.
She organized the work and asked questions when necessary.
She learned the importance of patience and organization.
cloze: Sophie received a very difficult ______ at work.
At first, the instructions felt very ______.
Sophie divided the project into smaller ______.
Lucas advised her to focus on one ______ at a time.
In the end, patience helped Sophie complete the ______.
speaking: Talk about a difficult task or project you experienced.
Why was it difficult?
Did you feel stressed or confused?
How did you solve the problem?
What did you learn afterward?
example: Last year, I had to complete a difficult project at school. At first, I felt overwhelmed because the instructions were complicated and there was a lot of work. Then, I organized the project into smaller parts and focused on one step at a time. Little by little, the task became easier, and I finally completed it successfully.
grammar: “At first, I did not know how to start.”
“I broke the task into smaller steps.”

title: Working Remotely
content: Working remotely has become much more common in recent years. Many companies now allow employees to work from home either full-time or part-time. For some people, remote work feels more comfortable and productive. For others, it creates isolation and communication difficulties.
Emma discovered both sides of remote work after her company introduced a flexible work policy.
At first, she was extremely happy about the change.
“No more crowded trains every morning,” she told Sophie excitedly.
“And no more waking up at 6 a.m.,” Sophie added with a laugh.
Emma could wake up later, organize her schedule more freely, and work in a quieter environment. She also saved time and energy by avoiding long daily transportation.
During the first weeks, she felt much more relaxed.

Her mornings became calmer, and she enjoyed preparing coffee quietly while starting work from her apartment. She concentrated better on some tasks because there were fewer interruptions than in the office.
Honestly, remote work felt almost perfect at the beginning.
One afternoon, Lucas called her during a break.
“So,” he asked, “are you enjoying working from home?”
“That’s what everyone says during the first month.”
At the time, Emma did not fully understand what he meant.
But little by little, things started changing.

After several weeks, Emma noticed that her days were becoming strangely repetitive. Some mornings, she barely left the apartment. Conversations with colleagues happened mostly through messages or online meetings.
At first, she thought this was not a problem.
But over time, the isolation became more noticeable.
One evening, after finishing work, Emma realized she had not spoken face-to-face with anyone all day.
The apartment suddenly felt very quiet.
She sighed softly and looked at her laptop.
“This is actually a little lonely,” she admitted to herself.

The following week during an online team meeting, communication problems also started appearing. Some discussions became confusing because people misunderstood messages or interrupted each other accidentally during video calls.
After one particularly frustrating meeting, Daniel spoke openly to the team.
“Remote work has advantages,” he explained, “but communication requires more effort now.”
Later that afternoon, Emma met Lucas for coffee after work.
“I love the flexibility,” she explained. “But sometimes I miss normal office interactions.”

Over the following months, Emma started testing different routines to create better balance. Instead of staying home every day, she sometimes worked from cafés or shared workspaces. She also made more effort to meet colleagues in person occasionally.
Emma realized that remote work itself was not the problem. The real challenge was finding the right balance between independence and human connection.
She also noticed that different people reacted differently to remote work.

For example, Sophie loved working from home because she concentrated better in quiet environments. Meanwhile, Nathan strongly preferred the office because he enjoyed teamwork and direct communication.
One afternoon during lunch at the office, the team started discussing the topic together.
“If I stay home too much,” Nathan admitted, “I lose motivation.”
“I’m the opposite. I work much better at home.”
“I think I’m somewhere in the middle.”
That conversation helped her realize something important:
There is no perfect solution for everyone.
Some people need social interaction to stay motivated. Others need quiet environments to focus effectively.
Now, Emma usually works remotely several days each week while still spending time at the office regularly. That combination helps her stay productive without feeling isolated.
One evening, while finishing work calmly from home, she reflected on everything she had learned.
Remote work offers freedom, flexibility, and comfort.
But people still need connection, communication, and balance too.
And in many ways, the ideal solution is not choosing completely between home and office…
but learning how to combine the advantages of both.
questions: Why did Emma enjoy remote work at first?
What problems appeared later?
Why did communication become more difficult?
What changes did Emma make to improve balance?
Why did Sophie and Nathan prefer different work environments?
What do you think are the advantages of remote work?
answers_q: She had more flexibility and less transportation stress.
She started feeling isolated and repetitive.
Online communication sometimes created misunderstandings.
She worked outside home more often and met colleagues regularly.
Sophie liked quiet environments, while Nathan preferred social interaction.
Example answer: Remote work offers flexibility and saves time.
cloze: Remote work offers more ______ in daily life.
After some time, Emma started feeling ______.
Emma tried creating better ______ in her routine.
In the end, she preferred a mix of home and ______ work.
speaking: Talk about working remotely or studying from home.
Do you prefer working at home or in an office?
What are the advantages?
What are the disadvantages?
How can people stay productive remotely?
example: I think remote work has many advantages because people save time and have more flexibility. However, working from home can sometimes feel isolating, especially without regular social interaction. Personally, I think a balance between remote work and office work is probably the best solution.
grammar: “On the other hand, it can be isolating.”
“In my opinion, a balance is ideal.”

title: Facing a Fear
content: A few months ago, Lucas decided to face one of his biggest fears: speaking in public.
For years, public speaking made him extremely nervous. Even simple situations at work felt stressful when he needed to explain ideas in front of several people.
Whenever meetings became too interactive, Lucas tried staying quiet.
If someone else volunteered to present, he immediately felt relieved.
At first, he believed this was not a serious problem.
“I’m just a quiet person,” he often told himself.
But little by little, he realized that fear was limiting him professionally and personally.
One afternoon, Daniel asked him to lead a short presentation during an important client meeting.
As soon as Lucas heard the request, his stomach tightened.
“You know the project well. You’re the best person to explain it.”
Lucas smiled politely, but inside, he immediately felt anxious.
For the next few days, he thought constantly about the presentation. Every time he imagined standing in front of the room, he felt stressed.

“What if I forget everything?”
 “What if people notice I’m nervous?”
 “What if I make mistakes?”
The fear became so strong that he even considered inventing an excuse to avoid the situation completely.
Later that week, he discussed the problem with Emma during lunch.
“I hate speaking in front of groups,” he admitted honestly.
“That’s because I try very hard to hide it.”
Emma stayed quiet for a moment before speaking.
“You know,” she said carefully, “avoiding fear usually makes it stronger.”
That evening, while thinking about the conversation, he realized something important:
If he continued avoiding public speaking forever, nothing would ever improve.
So instead of escaping the situation again, he decided to face the fear gradually.

At first, Lucas started practicing in very small situations. During team meetings, he forced himself to speak a little more instead of remaining silent. Sometimes he simply shared short opinions or asked questions.
His voice occasionally shook slightly, and he still became nervous before speaking.
But little by little, the fear became less intense.
A few weeks later, Sophie invited him to attend a small communication workshop after work. The group was relaxed and supportive, which helped Lucas feel safer practicing.
During one exercise, participants had to speak for two minutes about a personal experience.
At first, Lucas felt panic immediately.
“There’s no way I can do this,” he thought.
But then he remembered his goal.
So he stood up slowly and started speaking.
The beginning felt difficult. He spoke too quickly and forgot part of his sentence at one point. Still, he continued instead of stopping completely.
When he finished, something surprising happened.
After the session, Sophie smiled proudly.
“That’s normal,” Sophie replied. “Progress usually feels uncomfortable at first.”
Over the following months, Lucas continued practicing regularly. He volunteered for small presentations at work and focused on improving little by little instead of expecting perfection immediately.
Then finally, the important client presentation arrived.
Before entering the meeting room, Lucas still felt nervous. His hands were slightly cold, and his heart was beating faster than normal.
But this time, the fear no longer controlled him completely.
When the presentation started, he focused on speaking slowly and clearly instead of trying to appear perfect.
Of course, there were still small mistakes and moments of hesitation. But Lucas continued speaking calmly and successfully finished the presentation.
Afterward, Daniel smiled while leaving the room.
Later that evening, while walking home, he reflected on how much he had changed.
The fear had not disappeared completely.
But now, it no longer stopped him from acting.
Because Lucas finally understood something important:
Confidence is not the absence of fear.
Real confidence appears when people continue moving forward even while feeling afraid.
questions: Why was public speaking difficult for Lucas?
How did fear affect his professional life?
What advice did Emma give him?
Why did Lucas start practicing in small situations first?
What happened during the communication workshop?
What important lesson did Lucas learn?
answers_q: He became very nervous when speaking in front of groups.
He avoided presentations and interactive meetings.
She explained that avoiding fear makes it stronger.
Small situations felt less stressful and helped him improve gradually.
He completed a short speech successfully despite feeling nervous.
Confidence comes from acting despite fear.
cloze: Lucas was afraid of speaking in ______.

Why was it difficult?
How did you improve?
What did you learn from the experience?
example: A few years ago, I was afraid of speaking English in front of other people because I worried about making mistakes. At first, I avoided conversations whenever possible. Then, I started practicing little by little with friends and teachers. Over time, I became more comfortable and confident speaking publicly.
vocab: public speaking → prise de parole en public

title: Learning from Others
content: People often think learning only happens in schools, courses, or books. But in reality, many important lessons come from observing other people, listening carefully, and staying open to different perspectives.
Lucas understood this gradually during his first years at work.
At the beginning of his career, he preferred doing everything independently. He believed asking too many questions could make him look inexperienced or unprepared.
So whenever he received a task, he tried solving every problem alone.
At first, that approach seemed effective.
Lucas worked hard, completed projects seriously, and avoided depending on other people too much. He liked feeling autonomous and responsible.
However, over time, he started noticing something important.
Some colleagues worked more efficiently than him even when they seemed less stressed.
One afternoon, while preparing an important report, Lucas became frustrated because the task was taking much longer than expected.
Nearby, Sophie completed a similar project calmly and quickly.
“How do you finish these reports so fast?”
Instead of simply giving a short answer, Sophie showed him how she organized information, prioritized tasks, and prepared documents step by step.
Her method was much clearer and more efficient than his own.
For the first time, he realized that observing other people could teach him practical skills he would never discover alone.
Later that week, Emma helped him during a client presentation. Lucas noticed how naturally she communicated with people. She stayed calm, listened carefully, and explained complex ideas very clearly.
After the meeting, Lucas spoke with her privately.
“You always sound confident during presentations,” he said.
“Trust me, I wasn’t always like that.”
“Of course,” she replied. “I learned by watching experienced people and practicing little by little.”
That conversation changed Lucas’s perspective again.
At first, he believed successful people naturally had more talent or confidence. But slowly, he understood that many professional skills are learned through observation, feedback, and experience.
After that realization, Lucas became more open professionally.
Instead of trying to appear perfect all the time, he started asking more questions and paying closer attention to how experienced colleagues handled difficult situations.
Sometimes he observed Daniel during meetings with clients.
Daniel stayed calm under pressure, listened carefully before responding, and rarely reacted emotionally during stressful conversations.
One day after a difficult meeting, Lucas asked him:
“How do you stay so calm in situations like that?”
“Experience helps,” he admitted. “But listening carefully is probably the most important thing.”
Lucas reflected on that answer for a long time afterward.
Little by little, he started improving many aspects of his work:
Not because he copied other people completely…
but because he learned from their experiences and adapted useful ideas to his own style.
One afternoon during lunch, Nathan made an interesting comment.
“You ask a lot more questions now than before,” he observed.
“Yeah. I finally realized that asking questions is smarter than pretending to know everything.”
Lucas realized he was probably right.

As months passed, Lucas became more comfortable learning from different people around him. Sometimes valuable lessons came from managers. Other times, they came from colleagues, clients, or even younger employees with different perspectives.
He also understood another important thing:
People must accept that they do not know everything already.
One evening, while finishing work after a productive day, Lucas reflected on how much he had changed professionally.
At the beginning of his career, he wanted to prove he could succeed independently.
Now, he understood something much more valuable:
People improve faster when they stay curious, observe carefully, ask questions, and remain open to learning from others.
Because every person has different experiences, skills, mistakes, and perspectives to share.
And sometimes, one conversation or one observation can completely change the way someone works or thinks.
questions: Why did Lucas prefer working independently at first?
What did Sophie teach him?
Why did Emma inspire Lucas?
What did Lucas learn from observing Daniel?
Why did Lucas start asking more questions?
Why is learning from others important?
answers_q: He wanted to appear capable and independent.
She taught him a more organized and efficient method.
She communicated clearly and confidently during presentations.
He learned the importance of calm communication and listening.
He realized asking questions helps people improve faster.
Example answer: Other people can share valuable experience and ideas.
cloze: Sophie showed him a more efficient working ______.
Emma learned confidence through practice and ______.

example: I learned a lot from one of my teachers because they explained things very clearly and always stayed patient. By observing their methods, I improved my organization and communication skills. I think learning from experienced people can help us grow much faster.
grammar: “I learned a lot from my colleagues.”
“They showed me new ways of working.”

title: A Time I Failed
content: Failure is part of life, but accepting failure is often much more difficult than people expect.
Lucas learned this lesson last year after spending several months working on a project that ultimately failed.
At the beginning, the project seemed extremely promising.
Nathan had invited Lucas to help develop a new digital platform for small businesses. The idea was modern, useful, and full of potential. Everyone involved felt motivated and optimistic.
For the first few weeks, the team worked with a lot of energy. Lucas spent long evenings planning strategies, organizing meetings, and solving technical problems with Nathan.
Honestly, he felt excited about the future again.
“This project could really change things for us,” he told Emma one evening.
At the time, Lucas truly believed the project would succeed.
But little by little, problems started appearing.
Some technical issues took much longer to solve than expected. Communication between different partners became complicated. Certain deadlines were missed, and financial pressure slowly increased.
“Every project has challenges,” he told himself.
However, over time, the situation became more stressful.
The team started working harder and harder, but progress remained slow. Some clients lost interest, and unexpected expenses created additional pressure.
One afternoon during a meeting, Nathan looked exhausted.
“I don’t know how much longer we can continue like this,” he admitted quietly.
Still, he continued trying to fix the situation. He worked late regularly, searched for solutions constantly, and refused to accept the possibility of failure.
Part of him believed that enough effort could solve everything.
But unfortunately, reality became impossible to ignore.
Several months later, the team finally made a difficult decision:
The financial situation was too unstable, and continuing would create even more problems.
When Lucas heard the final decision, he felt deeply disappointed.
For weeks afterward, he kept thinking about everything differently.
“We wasted so much time,” he thought repeatedly.
At first, the failure affected his confidence strongly. He questioned his decisions, abilities, and professional judgment.
One evening, he met Sophie for coffee and spoke honestly about the situation.
“I feel like I failed completely,” he admitted.
“You failed at one project,” she replied calmly. “That’s not the same thing.”
“Of course,” Sophie said gently. “But failure doesn’t automatically mean the experience was useless.”
At first, Lucas struggled to believe that.
For several weeks, he focused only on disappointment and frustration. But little by little, his perspective started changing.
Instead of avoiding the experience emotionally, he decided to analyze it carefully.
What could have been done differently?
What lessons could help him in the future?
The more he reflected honestly, the more he realized the experience had taught him valuable things.
For example, he understood the importance of:
and asking for help earlier when problems appear.
He also discovered something personal about himself:
During difficult situations, he often tried controlling everything alone instead of sharing responsibilities effectively.
That realization was uncomfortable, but useful.
Several months later, Lucas discussed the experience again with Daniel during a professional conversation.
“I still think about that project sometimes,” Lucas admitted.
“That’s normal. Important failures usually stay in people’s minds.”
“I just wish things had ended differently.”
“Maybe. But failure can also teach lessons that success never teaches.”
That sentence stayed in Lucas’s mind afterward.
Slowly, he stopped seeing the experience as wasted time.
But the experience also helped him become more realistic, more patient, and more aware of how to manage future challenges.
Now, when Lucas faces difficult situations, he reacts differently. He no longer expects everything to succeed perfectly immediately.
It shows people what needs to improve, what needs to change, and how they can grow stronger afterward.
And sometimes, the lessons learned from failure become more valuable than success itself.
questions: Why did Lucas feel excited about the project at first?
What problems appeared during the project?
Why did the team decide to stop the project?
How did Lucas feel after the failure?
What important lessons did Lucas learn?
Why can failure be useful sometimes?
answers_q: The project seemed promising and motivating.
They faced technical, financial, and communication problems.
The situation became financially unstable.
He felt disappointed and questioned himself.
He learned about communication, planning, and teamwork.
Example answer: Failure helps people learn and improve.
cloze: Lucas worked on the project for several ______.
